Option D (Proliferative DR) is INCORRECT because proliferative DR requires the presence of neovascularisation (new vessels on the disc or elsewhere). The fundus here shows no new vessels — this is pre-proliferative/non-proliferative DR. Option E (AMD) is INCORRECT because AMD shows drusen (pale yellow deposits at the macula), geographic atrophy, or choroidal neovascularisation membranes, not the microaneurysms and haemorrhages of diabetic retinopathy.

label: Papilloedema
stem: A 26-year-old obese woman presents with a 3-month history of daily headaches, worse on lying flat, pulsatile tinnitus, and transient visual obscurations lasting seconds. Visual acuity is preserved at 6/6. The fundoscopy shown demonstrates bilateral optic disc swelling with blurred disc margins, haemorrhages at the disc edge, and loss of physiological cup. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
options: Visual field testing alone and reassurance
MRI brain with gadolinium and MR venography, followed by lumbar puncture if imaging is normal
Urgent ophthalmology referral for laser treatment of the optic discs
Temporal artery biopsy to exclude giant cell arteritis
Carotid Doppler ultrasound
correctAnswerText: MRI brain with gadolinium and MR venography, followed by lumbar puncture if imaging is normal
explanation: This obese young woman with headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations, and bilateral papilloedema has the classic presentation of idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH, pseudotumour cerebri). Investigation must exclude secondary causes before confirming the diagnosis. Option B (MRI brain + gadolinium + MR venography, then LP if imaging normal) is CORRECT NG228. MRI with gadolinium is performed first to exclude secondary causes of raised ICP: space-occupying lesions, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, meningeal carcinomatosis. MR venography assesses for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (particularly relevant in obese women). If imaging is normal, lumbar puncture measures opening CSF pressure (>25 cmH₂O in IIH, often 30–50 cmH₂O), confirms diagnosis, and provides therapeutic pressure reduction. Option A (Visual field testing alone) is INCORRECT because visual field testing is important for monitoring IIH but does not establish the diagnosis or exclude secondary causes. The history warrants urgent full investigation. Option C (Laser treatment of optic discs) is INCORRECT because the optic disc swelling is from raised ICP — laser treatment of the disc would have no therapeutic effect on the underlying pressure and no role in IIH management. Option D (Temporal artery biopsy) is INCORRECT because giant cell arteritis is a disease of >50-year-olds with jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, and elevated ESR. This 26-year-old's fundoscopic findings represent papilloedema from raised ICP, not ischaemic optic neuropathy from GCA. Option E (Carotid Doppler) is INCORRECT because pulsatile tinnitus in IIH results from pressure transmitted to the venous sinuses, not carotid stenosis. Carotid Doppler does not investigate raised ICP.

label: Sickle Cell Disease
stem: A 25-year-old man of West African origin presents with severe pain in his lower back and bilateral femur, fever of 38.8°C, and worsening anaemia. He has a known haemoglobinopathy. The blood film shown demonstrates sickle-shaped (crescent/holly-leaf) red cells, target cells, and Howell-Jolly bodies (nuclear remnants indicating hyposplenia). Hb is 62 g/L, reticulocytes are elevated at 18%. What is the most appropriate management for this acute vaso-occlusive crisis?
options: Urgent bone marrow biopsy to exclude aplastic crisis
IV iron infusion (ferric carboxymaltose)
IV morphine, aggressive IV fluids, supplemental oxygen, and haematology review — with urgent exchange transfusion if indicated
Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) starting dose 15 mg/kg/day
Platelet transfusion for thrombocytopaenia
correctAnswerText: IV morphine, aggressive IV fluids, supplemental oxygen, and haematology review — with urgent exchange transfusion if indicated
explanation: This 25-year-old with sickle cell disease (evidenced by the blood film) is having an acute vaso-occlusive crisis (painful sickle crisis): severe bone pain, fever, and worsening anaemia. Immediate pain management and supportive care are the priorities. Option C (IV morphine, IV fluids, O₂, haematology review, exchange transfusion if indicated) is CORRECT NG143. Acute sickle crisis management: (1) Analgesia — oral or IV morphine within 30 minutes of arrival (patient controlled analgesia if possible); (2) Oxygen if SpO₂ <95%; (3) IV fluids for hydration; (4) Fever/infection assessment — sickle patients are functionally asplenic (Howell-Jolly bodies confirm hyposplenia) and vulnerable to encapsulated organisms (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae); (5) Exchange transfusion is indicated in acute chest syndrome, stroke, or severe sequestration. Option A (Bone marrow biopsy) is INCORRECT as the immediate step. Aplastic crisis (from parvovirus B19) causes a sudden drop in Hb with absent reticulocytes — this patient has elevated reticulocytes (18%), indicating normal bone marrow response. Option B (IV iron infusion) is INCORRECT because sickle cell patients are not iron deficient — they have a haemolytic anaemia with normal or elevated iron stores. Iron supplementation could worsen iron overload from repeated transfusions. Option D (Hydroxycarbamide) is INCORRECT as the acute management. Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) is used for prophylaxis of recurrent sickling crises by increasing HbF levels, but is not a treatment for an acute crisis. Option E (Platelet transfusion) is INCORRECT because there is no thrombocytopaenia mentioned. Platelet transfusion does not address vaso-occlusion.

label: Iron Deficiency Blood Film
stem: A 42-year-old woman with a history of menorrhagia presents with fatigue and breathlessness on climbing stairs. FBC: Hb 81 g/L, MCV 66 fL, MCH 20 pg, RDW elevated. The blood film shown demonstrates microcytic hypochromic red cells, pencil cells (elongated pencil-shaped red cells), anisocytosis (variation in cell size), and a large area of central pallor (>1/3 of red cell diameter). Serum ferritin is 4 mcg/L. What does this blood film most likely demonstrate?
options: Thalassaemia trait — target cells with near-normal iron stores
Anaemia of chronic disease — normocytic normochromic cells
Iron deficiency anaemia — microcytic hypochromic cells with pencil cells
Sideroblastic anaemia — dimorphic red cell population
Vitamin B12 deficiency — macrocytic oval cells with hypersegmented neutrophils
correctAnswerText: Iron deficiency anaemia — microcytic hypochromic cells with pencil cells
explanation: The blood film shows iron deficiency anaemia (IDA): microcytic hypochromic red cells with central pallor >1/3 of diameter, pencil cells (elliptocytes/poikilocytes), and increased anisocytosis (RDW elevated). Combined with low ferritin (4 mcg/L), MCV 66, and menorrhagia, this is classic IDA. Option C (Iron deficiency anaemia — microcytic hypochromic with pencil cells) is CORRECT. IDA results from insufficient iron for haem synthesis, causing small, poorly haemoglobinised red cells. Pencil cells are elongated deformed cells from the thin cell membrane of iron-deficient cells. Management: identify and treat the cause (here: investigate and treat menorrhagia), oral ferrous sulfate 200 mg three times daily for at least 3 months (continue for 3 months after Hb normalises to replenish stores) CKS. Option A (Thalassaemia trait) is INCORRECT because thalassaemia trait causes microcytosis with near-normal or elevated red cell count, target cells, and NORMAL ferritin. The very low ferritin (4 mcg/L) indicates true iron deficiency, not thalassaemia. Mentzer index (MCV/RBC): <13 favours thalassaemia, >13 favours IDA. Option B (Anaemia of chronic disease) is INCORRECT because ACD causes normocytic or mildly microcytic anaemia with elevated ferritin (stored iron), reduced serum iron and TIBC. The very low ferritin excludes ACD. Option D (Sideroblastic anaemia) is INCORRECT because sideroblastic anaemia shows a dimorphic blood film (two populations: normal and microcytic hypochromic) with elevated ferritin and ring sideroblasts on bone marrow iron staining. Option E (B12 deficiency) is INCORRECT because B12 deficiency causes macrocytic (not microcytic) anaemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and macro-ovalocytes. MCV would be elevated, not reduced.

label: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
stem: A 72-year-old man presents with progressive bilateral hearing loss over 6 years, worse for high-frequency sounds and in noisy environments. He also reports tinnitus. He denies vertigo, ear pain, or discharge. The pure-tone audiogram shown demonstrates bilateral symmetrical high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss with air-bone gap absent (air conduction = bone conduction thresholds), with thresholds of 60 dB at 4000 Hz and 80 dB at 8000 Hz. What does this audiogram most likely demonstrate?
options: Bilateral conductive hearing loss from otosclerosis — elevated bone conduction thresholds
Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss from acoustic neuroma
Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss consistent with age-related hearing loss (presbycusis)
Mixed hearing loss from chronic otitis media with effusion
Noise-induced hearing loss at 4 kHz notch — occupational history required
correctAnswerText: Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss consistent with age-related hearing loss (presbycusis)
explanation: The audiogram shows bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) with no air-bone gap — characteristic of presbycusis (age-related hearing loss). The symmetric downward sloping audiogram pattern with loss greatest at high frequencies is pathognomonic. Option C (Bilateral SNHL consistent with presbycusis) is CORRECT. Presbycusis is the most common cause of hearing loss in older adults, caused by cumulative loss of outer hair cells in the cochlea (predominantly basal turn, responsible for high-frequency hearing). Key features: bilateral, symmetric, high-frequency SNHL, no air-bone gap. Management NG98: referral to audiology for bilateral digital hearing aids. Hearing aids improve quality of life significantly and reduce risk of social isolation and cognitive decline. Option A (Conductive hearing loss from otosclerosis) is INCORRECT because otosclerosis causes conductive hearing loss at low frequencies with a characteristic Carhart notch at 2000 Hz and an air-bone gap on audiogram (bone conduction better than air conduction). Otosclerosis typically presents in younger women. Option B (Unilateral acoustic neuroma) is INCORRECT because acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) causes asymmetric (typically unilateral) SNHL, often with associated tinnitus and/or vertigo. The bilateral symmetric pattern here is inconsistent with acoustic neuroma. Option D (Mixed hearing loss from COME) is INCORRECT because chronic otitis media with effusion causes conductive hearing loss (air-bone gap present) with flat or gently sloping audiogram, not the high-frequency SNHL pattern of presbycusis. Option E (Noise-induced hearing loss) is INCORRECT because noise-induced SNHL characteristically shows a "4000 Hz notch" (isolated dip at 4 kHz) with recovery at higher frequencies. The progressive downsloping loss without a notch in this 72-year-old is more consistent with presbycusis, though an occupational history is relevant.

label: Failure To Thrive
stem: A 3-year-old boy is referred by his GP with faltering growth (previously called failure to thrive). The growth chart shown demonstrates his weight was on the 50th centile at birth and 6 months, but has progressively fallen through centiles — now tracking below the 2nd centile at age 3 years. He has chronic diarrhoea, abdominal distension, and was miserable. He eats poorly. His parents are of normal height. What is the most appropriate initial investigation?
options: Bone age X-ray to assess growth potential
Karyotype analysis to exclude Turner syndrome
Full blood count, coeliac serology (anti-tTG IgA), and IgA level
Growth hormone stimulation test
Renal function, urine dipstick and culture
correctAnswerText: Full blood count, coeliac serology (anti-tTG IgA), and IgA level
explanation: This 3-year-old has organic faltering growth with a crossed centile pattern (downward deviation across centiles), diarrhoea, abdominal distension, and irritability — a classic presentation of coeliac disease. Option C (FBC, anti-tTG IgA, and serum IgA level) is CORRECT NG20 and NG75. Coeliac disease is the most common organic cause of faltering growth with GI symptoms in this age group. The anti-tTG IgA is the primary screening test; total IgA is measured simultaneously as IgA deficiency (present in 2–3% of coeliac patients) causes false-negative anti-tTG IgA results. FBC checks for iron deficiency anaemia (a common associated finding in coeliac disease). If anti-tTG IgA >10× upper limit of normal AND HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive, diagnosis can be made without duodenal biopsy in children. Option A (Bone age X-ray) is INCORRECT as the first investigation. Bone age is useful for constitutional growth delay or growth hormone deficiency, where there is consistent short stature without GI symptoms or centile crossing. This child has clear organic symptoms warranting coeliac investigation first. Option B (Karyotype for Turner syndrome) is INCORRECT because Turner syndrome causes short stature in girls, not boys. This patient is male. Option D (Growth hormone stimulation test) is INCORRECT as first-line because GH deficiency is associated with proportionate short stature, reduced growth velocity, and normal GI findings — not the GI symptoms and centile crossing seen here. Option E (Renal function and urine culture) is INCORRECT as the first-line investigation, though chronic renal failure can cause faltering growth. The dominant GI features here point to a GI cause, making coeliac disease the primary diagnosis to exclude.

label: Ctg Late Decelerations
stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at 39 weeks gestation is in active labour. She has pre-eclampsia with BP 155/98 mmHg on labetalol. The CTG shown demonstrates a baseline fetal heart rate of 155 bpm, reduced variability (<5 bpm for >90 minutes), recurrent late decelerations (decelerations beginning after the peak of each contraction and returning to baseline after the contraction ends), and a rising baseline. The woman is 6 cm dilated. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Continue monitoring and reassess in 2 hours
Augmentation with IV oxytocin to expedite labour
Immediate category 1 caesarean section (decision-to-delivery <30 minutes)
Fetal blood sampling (FBS) to assess fetal scalp pH
Change maternal position to left lateral, stop oxytocin, and give high-flow oxygen
correctAnswerText: Immediate category 1 caesarean section (decision-to-delivery <30 minutes)
explanation: This CTG shows pathological features indicating acute fetal compromise: recurrent late decelerations (uteroplacental insufficiency), reduced variability <5 bpm for >90 minutes, and a rising baseline — this is a pathological CTG requiring immediate delivery. Option C (Immediate category 1 caesarean section — <30 minutes) is CORRECT CG190 and FIGO CTG interpretation guidelines. Late decelerations with reduced variability and a rising baseline in a pre-eclamptic mother constitute a pathological CTG requiring immediate action. The combination of reduced variability and repetitive late decelerations is a category 1 indication (immediate threat to fetal or maternal life). At 6 cm dilation, caesarean section is the fastest route to delivery. FBS would be contraindicated in pre-eclampsia as the mother cannot safely flex for the procedure under these circumstances. Option A (Continue monitoring for 2 hours) is INCORRECT because pathological CTG changes (late decelerations with reduced variability) cannot be watched for 2 hours. Fetal acidosis progresses rapidly and permanent neurological injury can result from delay. Option B (Augment with oxytocin) is INCORRECT because oxytocin increases uterine contraction frequency/amplitude, reducing uteroplacental blood flow further and worsening late decelerations. Oxytocin must be stopped, not started, in this situation. Option D (Fetal blood sampling) is INCORRECT in this scenario because the CTG shows multiple pathological features (late decels + reduced variability + rising baseline) — immediate delivery is indicated without FBS. FBS is appropriate for equivocal CTG findings. Option E (Left lateral, stop oxytocin, O₂) is INCORRECT as the definitive response. These are temporising measures in acute decelerations (cord compression) but do not address persistent pathological CTG from uteroplacental insufficiency, which requires immediate delivery.

label: Dress Syndrome
stem: A 38-year-old man with epilepsy developed a widespread morbilliform rash 4 weeks after starting carbamazepine. He now has fever (38.9°C), facial oedema, cervical lymphadenopathy, and hepatomegaly. The blood film shown demonstrates marked peripheral eosinophilia with atypical lymphocytes. FBC: WBC 18 × 10⁹/L, eosinophils 3.2 × 10⁹/L. ALT is 380 IU/L. What does this blood film most likely demonstrate, and what is the most important immediate management?
options: Infectious mononucleosis — test for EBV antibodies and manage conservatively
Eosinophilia consistent with DRESS syndrome — immediately stop carbamazepine and start high-dose oral prednisolone
Hypereosinophilic syndrome — start imatinib
Atopic dermatitis exacerbation — topical corticosteroids and antihistamines
Leukaemia — bone marrow biopsy urgently
correctAnswerText: Eosinophilia consistent with DRESS syndrome — immediately stop carbamazepine and start high-dose oral prednisolone
explanation:
The blood film shows eosinophilia with atypical lymphocytes in a patient with fever, rash, facial oedema, lymphadenopathy, and hepatitis starting 4 weeks after carbamazepine — this is the classic presentation of DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms).

Option B (DRESS syndrome — stop carbamazepine + high-dose oral prednisolone) is CORRECT. DRESS is a potentially life-threatening drug hypersensitivity reaction characterised by: skin rash (often morbilliform), fever, lymphadenopathy, facial oedema, internal organ involvement (hepatitis, nephritis, pneumonitis), and eosinophilia/atypical lymphocytes. RegiSCAR score confirms diagnosis. Aromatic antiepileptics (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine) are a common cause. Immediate drug withdrawal is essential; systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg) reduce inflammation and organ damage. The reaction can persist or worsen weeks after drug cessation.

Option A (Infectious mononucleosis) is INCORRECT because EBV causes fever, lymphadenopathy, and atypical lymphocytes — but without eosinophilia and specifically without the temporal relationship to a new drug. Hepatitis occurs in EBV, but the 4-week drug timeline and eosinophilia point to DRESS.

Option C (Hypereosinophilic syndrome) is INCORRECT because HES requires eosinophils >1.5 × 10⁹/L for >6 months with end-organ damage in the absence of an identifiable cause. Here the cause is clearly carbamazepine-induced DRESS.

Option D (Atopic dermatitis exacerbation) is INCORRECT because atopic dermatitis does not cause systemic features of fever, lymphadenopathy, hepatitis, and eosinophilia. This is not an atopic reaction.

Option E (Leukaemia) is INCORRECT because leukaemia causes anaemia, thrombocytopaenia, and blast cells on blood film. The clinical context — drug exposure, rash, organ involvement — makes DRESS the correct diagnosis.

label: Hearing Loss
stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of progressive bilateral hearing loss, worse on the left. She reports no tinnitus, ear discharge, or vertigo. Otoscopy shows intact tympanic membranes bilaterally with a normal light reflex on the right and a slightly dull reflex on the left. The audiogram shown demonstrates the pattern above. What type of hearing loss does this audiogram demonstrate, and what is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
options: Sensorineural hearing loss — most likely presbycusis
Conductive hearing loss — most likely otosclerosis
Mixed hearing loss — most likely chronic suppurative otitis media
Sensorineural hearing loss — most likely noise-induced hearing loss
Conductive hearing loss — most likely acoustic neuroma
correctAnswerText: Conductive hearing loss — most likely otosclerosis
explanation:
The audiogram shows reduced air conduction (AC) thresholds in both ears (hearing loss present) with normal bone conduction (BC) thresholds at 0 dB HL. This air-bone gap — where AC is impaired but BC is preserved — is the hallmark of conductive hearing loss. The pattern is bilateral and worse at all frequencies on the left.

Option B (Conductive hearing loss — otosclerosis) is CORRECT. Otosclerosis is the most common cause of progressive bilateral conductive hearing loss in adults aged 30–50, more common in women. It involves abnormal bone remodelling fixing the stapes footplate in the oval window, preventing transmission of sound vibrations. BC is preserved because the cochlea is unaffected. Classic features: bilateral, progressive, often worse in one ear, no discharge or vertigo, normal otoscopy (or Schwartze sign — flamingo pink blush through tympanic membrane). Treatment: surgical stapedectomy or hearing aids.

Option A (Sensorineural — presbycusis) is INCORRECT. Presbycusis causes sensorineural hearing loss: both AC and BC are reduced equally, with no air-bone gap. It also typically shows high-frequency loss first.

Option C (Mixed hearing loss) is INCORRECT. Mixed hearing loss would show both AC and BC reduced, with AC worse than BC. Here, BC is completely normal.

Option D (Sensorineural — noise-induced) is INCORRECT. Noise-induced hearing loss is sensorineural, characterised by a 4 kHz notch on audiogram with equal AC and BC reduction.

Option E (Acoustic neuroma) is INCORRECT. Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) causes unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, typically with tinnitus and sometimes vertigo. It does not produce a conductive pattern with air-bone gap.

label: Impetigo
stem: A mother brings her 5-year-old son to the GP. She noticed honey-coloured crusted lesions around his nose and mouth 3 days ago, which have since spread to his cheek. He is otherwise well with no fever. He attends nursery. The lesions are mildly itchy and the child has been scratching. There is no lymphadenopathy. The image shows the clinical findings. What is the most likely diagnosis and the first-line treatment?
options: Herpes simplex (cold sores) — topical aciclovir
Impetigo — topical fusidic acid
Contact dermatitis — topical hydrocortisone
Tinea faciei — topical clotrimazole
Eczema herpeticum — urgent oral aciclovir
correctAnswerText: Impetigo — topical fusidic acid
explanation: The image shows honey-coloured (golden) crusted lesions around the perioral and nasal region of a child — the classic presentation of non-bullous impetigo. Option B (Impetigo — topical fusidic acid) is CORRECT. Impetigo is the most common bacterial skin infection in children. Non-bullous impetigo (80% of cases) is caused by Staphylococcus aureus and/or Streptococcus pyogenes. It begins as small vesicles that rupture, leaving the characteristic golden-yellow crusting. Lesions spread by autoinoculation (scratching) and are highly contagious — the child should be excluded from nursery until the lesions are crusted over or 48 hours after starting antibiotics. topical fusidic acid (3 times daily for 5 days) as first-line for localised non-bullous impetigo. Oral flucloxacillin is used if widespread or systemic features. Option A (Herpes simplex) is INCORRECT. HSV cold sores cause grouped vesicles on an erythematous base, typically recurrent at the vermilion border. They do not produce the golden crusting seen here, and the spreading pattern is more consistent with bacterial impetigo. Option C (Contact dermatitis) is INCORRECT. Contact dermatitis causes an eczematous, weeping rash in a distribution corresponding to a contactant. It does not produce isolated honey-coloured crusted lesions. Option D (Tinea faciei) is INCORRECT. Tinea faciei causes an annular, scaly, erythematous plaque with a raised edge and central clearing. It does not produce honey-coloured crusting. Option E (Eczema herpeticum) is INCORRECT. Eczema herpeticum is a serious HSV superinfection of eczematous skin, causing punched-out erosions with fever and systemic upset. This child is well and does not have background eczema.

label: Inferior Stemi
stem: A 62-year-old man with hypertension presents with severe central chest pain radiating to the jaw for 90 minutes. He is sweaty and nauseated. His BP is 95/60 mmHg, HR 54 bpm. The ECG is shown. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Give aspirin 300 mg and arrange urgent PCI
Give IV furosemide and monitor closely
Perform echocardiogram before treatment
Start IV heparin infusion and await troponin
Give GTN spray and discharge if pain resolves
correctAnswerText: Give aspirin 300 mg and arrange urgent PCI
explanation: The ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL — diagnostic of inferior STEMI, most commonly caused by right coronary artery occlusion. Option A is CORRECT. Inferior STEMI requires immediate reperfusion. Aspirin 300 mg (loading dose) plus urgent PCI within 120 minutes of first medical contact is the standard of care NG185. The bradycardia and hypotension suggest right ventricular infarction (common with inferior MI) — avoid nitrates as they can cause catastrophic BP drop in RV infarction. Option B is INCORRECT — furosemide is contraindicated; RV infarction requires IV fluids to maintain preload, not diuresis. Option C is INCORRECT — echocardiography should not delay reperfusion therapy. Option D is INCORRECT — heparin alone is insufficient; mechanical reperfusion is superior to anticoagulation alone. Option E is INCORRECT — GTN is contraindicated in suspected RV infarction and STEMI requires definitive reperfusion.

label: Atrial Fibrillation
stem: A 71-year-old woman presents with palpitations and mild breathlessness. She has a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes. The ECG shows an irregularly irregular rhythm at 110 bpm with no discernible P waves. What is the most important next step in management?
options: Calculate CHA₂DS₂-VASc score and initiate anticoagulation if ≥2
Perform DC cardioversion immediately
Start digoxin to restore sinus rhythm
Arrange 24-hour Holter monitoring before any treatment
Prescribe aspirin 75 mg daily for stroke prevention
correctAnswerText: Calculate CHA₂DS₂-VASc score and initiate anticoagulation if ≥2
explanation: This ECG is diagnostic of atrial fibrillation (AF) — irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline. Option A is CORRECT. In newly diagnosed AF, stroke risk assessment using CHA₂DS₂-VASc is the priority. This patient scores at least 3 (age ≥75 = 2 points, hypertension = 1, female sex = 1, diabetes = 1) — well above the threshold for anticoagulation with a DOAC (e.g. apixaban, rivaroxaban) NG196. Option B is INCORRECT — emergency DC cardioversion is only indicated for haemodynamically unstable AF. This patient is stable. Option C is INCORRECT — digoxin does not cardiovert AF and is second-line for rate control. Option D is INCORRECT — diagnosis is already confirmed on the presenting ECG. Holter monitoring is for paroxysmal arrhythmias. Option E is INCORRECT — aspirin is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in AF; DOACs are significantly superior.

label: Ventricular Tachycardia
stem: A 58-year-old man with a previous MI collapses in the ED waiting area. He is conscious but pale, BP 80/50 mmHg, HR 180 bpm. The ECG shows broad complex tachycardia with AV dissociation. What is the immediate management?
options: Synchronised DC cardioversion at 200 J
IV adenosine 6 mg bolus
IV verapamil 5 mg
IV amiodarone 300 mg over 10–20 minutes
IV lidocaine 1 mg/kg
correctAnswerText: Synchronised DC cardioversion at 200 J
explanation:
Broad complex tachycardia with AV dissociation and haemodynamic compromise in a patient with prior MI is ventricular tachycardia (VT) until proven otherwise.

Option A is CORRECT. Haemodynamically unstable VT (BP 80/50, impaired consciousness) requires immediate synchronised DC cardioversion per ALS/Resus UK guidelines. Start at 200 J biphasic, sedation if time allows.

Option B is INCORRECT — adenosine is appropriate for SVT. In VT it can cause VF. AV dissociation (independent P waves and QRS) confirms VT not SVT with aberrancy.

Option C is INCORRECT — verapamil in VT can cause circulatory arrest. It is absolutely contraindicated in broad complex tachycardia of uncertain origin.

Option D is INCORRECT — amiodarone is correct for stable VT but this patient is haemodynamically compromised; electrical cardioversion takes priority.

Option E is INCORRECT — lidocaine is an alternative for stable VT but again cardioversion is needed here first.

label: Complete Heart Block
stem: A 74-year-old man is brought in by ambulance after a syncopal episode. He is drowsy, BP 90/60 mmHg, pulse 34 bpm. His medications include atenolol and diltiazem for hypertension. The ECG demonstrates complete dissociation between P waves and QRS complexes, with narrow escape rhythm at 34 bpm. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: IV atropine 500 mcg and prepare for transcutaneous pacing
Stop atenolol and diltiazem, observe on monitored bed
IV isoprenaline infusion
Oral theophylline and outpatient referral
Emergency permanent pacemaker insertion
correctAnswerText: IV atropine 500 mcg and prepare for transcutaneous pacing
explanation:
This ECG shows complete (third-degree) heart block — P waves and QRS complexes have no relationship to each other. The narrow escape rhythm suggests a junctional escape, but HR 34 bpm with haemodynamic compromise requires immediate treatment.

Option A is CORRECT. First-line treatment for symptomatic complete heart block is IV atropine 500 mcg (up to 3 mg total) while arranging transcutaneous pacing. The medication history of atenolol + diltiazem is a key clue — this drug combination causes additive AV node suppression and may be the precipitant.

Option B is INCORRECT — while stopping the offending drugs is appropriate, this alone is insufficient for a haemodynamically compromised patient.

Option C is INCORRECT — isoprenaline is no longer routinely used; transcutaneous pacing is preferred.

Option D is INCORRECT — oral theophylline is used only in chronic complete heart block awaiting pacing.

Option E is INCORRECT — permanent pacing is appropriate after stabilisation, not as an emergency first step.

label: Lbbb
stem: A 67-year-old man with known heart failure presents with sudden-onset chest pain and dyspnoea. His ECG shows broad QRS complexes (>120 ms) with a dominant S wave in V1, broad notched R wave in V5–V6, and discordant ST changes. This is a new ECG finding compared to 6 months ago. What is the significance of this finding?
options: New LBBB in the context of chest pain is equivalent to STEMI and requires urgent reperfusion
LBBB is a normal finding in elderly patients and requires no action
LBBB indicates left ventricular hypertrophy — start antihypertensives
LBBB rules out myocardial infarction as the cause of chest pain
Rate-control medication should be started immediately
correctAnswerText: New LBBB in the context of chest pain is equivalent to STEMI and requires urgent reperfusion
explanation: The ECG shows left bundle branch block (LBBB) — broad QRS >120 ms, dominant S in V1, notched R in lateral leads, and secondary ST/T changes. Crucially, this is NEW. Option A is CORRECT. New LBBB in the context of ischaemic symptoms is treated as a STEMI equivalent and triggers the same urgent reperfusion pathway (primary PCI) NG185 and ESC guidelines. LBBB masks the typical ST changes of MI, making diagnosis of co-existing infarction difficult using standard ECG criteria alone. The Sgarbossa criteria can help identify STEMI in LBBB. Option B is INCORRECT — new LBBB is never a normal finding to be dismissed. Option C is INCORRECT — LBBB morphology is distinct from LVH pattern (tall R waves, strain pattern). Option D is INCORRECT — LBBB does not rule out MI; it makes ECG diagnosis harder. Option E is INCORRECT — rate control is irrelevant here; the patient is in sinus rhythm with a conduction defect.

label: Svt
stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset palpitations and mild dizziness for 45 minutes. She is haemodynamically stable. Her ECG shows a narrow complex tachycardia at 185 bpm with no visible P waves. Vagal manoeuvres (Valsalva) have failed. What is the next most appropriate treatment?
options: IV adenosine 6 mg rapid bolus
IV verapamil 5 mg
Synchronised DC cardioversion at 100 J
IV metoprolol 5 mg
IV digoxin loading dose
correctAnswerText: IV adenosine 6 mg rapid bolus
explanation: This ECG shows supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) — narrow complex regular tachycardia at 185 bpm with absent P waves (likely concealed in QRS in AVNRT). The patient is haemodynamically stable. Option A is CORRECT. After failed vagal manoeuvres, IV adenosine 6 mg rapid IV bolus (followed by 12 mg if no response) is first-line pharmacological treatment for stable SVT per Resus UK and. Adenosine blocks the AV node transiently, terminating re-entrant tachycardias. Warn the patient of transient chest tightness and flushing. Option B is INCORRECT — verapamil is an acceptable alternative but not first-line in the UK and should not be given if adenosine has been used or if broad complex tachycardia is possible. Option C is INCORRECT — DC cardioversion is reserved for haemodynamically unstable SVT. Option D is INCORRECT — IV metoprolol can terminate SVT but is slower acting and less effective than adenosine. Option E is INCORRECT — digoxin has a slow onset and is not used for acute SVT termination.

label: Cardiomegaly
stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of hypertension presents with progressive dyspnoea and bilateral ankle swelling. His chest X-ray is shown. The cardiothoracic ratio is measured at 0.62. What is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
options: Dilated cardiomyopathy
Acute pericarditis
Right-sided pneumothorax
Pulmonary fibrosis
Aortic dissection
correctAnswerText: Dilated cardiomyopathy
explanation:
The CXR shows cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on a PA film), in this case 0.62, with bilateral pleural effusions consistent with biventricular heart failure. The clinical context (progressive dyspnoea, ankle oedema, hypertension) points to dilated cardiomyopathy.

Option A is CORRECT. Dilated cardiomyopathy causes global cardiac enlargement visible as cardiomegaly on CXR, accompanied by signs of pulmonary venous congestion. Hypertension is a major risk factor. Echocardiography would confirm dilated, poorly contracting ventricles.

Option B is INCORRECT — acute pericarditis causes a "globular" pericardial effusion (flask-shaped), but would not cause ankle oedema acutely.

Option C is INCORRECT — pneumothorax causes lung field abnormalities, not cardiomegaly.

Option D is INCORRECT — pulmonary fibrosis causes interstitial lung changes and may cause cor pulmonale, but global cardiomegaly is not typical.

Option E is INCORRECT — aortic dissection causes mediastinal widening, not cardiomegaly.

label: Pleural Effusion
stem: A 67-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history presents with progressive dyspnoea and a 5 kg weight loss over 3 months. He has no fever. His CXR shows a large right-sided homogeneous opacity with a meniscus sign and mediastinal shift towards the left. What is the most important next investigation?
options: Diagnostic pleural aspiration with cytology
CT pulmonary angiogram
Pleural biopsy under ultrasound guidance
Bronchoscopy with BAL
Serum LDH and protein for Light's criteria only
correctAnswerText: Diagnostic pleural aspiration with cytology
explanation:
The CXR shows a right-sided pleural effusion with mediastinal shift away from the effusion (massive effusion). The clinical context — elderly male smoker, weight loss, no fever — raises strong concern for malignant effusion.

Option A is CORRECT. Diagnostic pleural aspiration (50 mL) sent for protein, LDH, cytology, pH, glucose and microbiological culture is the essential first investigation. Malignant cells on cytology have ~60% sensitivity for malignant effusion. Ultrasound guidance reduces complication rates.

Option B is INCORRECT — CTPA is for pulmonary embolism; an effusion this large is not explained by PE alone.

Option C is INCORRECT — pleural biopsy is indicated if aspiration cytology is non-diagnostic and malignancy remains suspected, not as first-line.

Option D is INCORRECT — bronchoscopy is indicated if there is a central endobronchial lesion, not for isolated effusion.

Option E is INCORRECT — Light's criteria alone distinguish exudate from transudate but do not identify the cause. Cytology is essential.

label: Pulmonary Embolism
stem: A 45-year-old woman on the combined oral contraceptive pill presents 5 days after a long-haul flight with sudden dyspnoea and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. HR 110 bpm, SpO₂ 93% on air. Her CTPA is shown, demonstrating filling defects in the right main pulmonary artery. What is the first-line treatment?
options: Rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days, then 20 mg once daily
IV unfractionated heparin infusion
Subcutaneous low-molecular-weight heparin only for 10 days
Thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg IV
Emergency pulmonary embolectomy
correctAnswerText: Rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days, then 20 mg once daily
explanation: The CTPA confirms pulmonary embolism (PE) with filling defects in the right main pulmonary artery. The patient is haemodynamically stable (no hypotension, no syncope). Option A is CORRECT. For non-massive PE, NG158 recommends direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) as first-line. Rivaroxaban is given as 15 mg BD for 21 days (loading phase), then 20 mg OD for at least 3 months. No parenteral heparin bridging is required. Option B is INCORRECT — IV unfractionated heparin is reserved for massive PE (haemodynamic instability), renal failure, or when parenteral route is needed. Option C is INCORRECT — LMWH alone for 10 days is insufficient; anticoagulation for PE should continue for at least 3 months. Option D is INCORRECT — thrombolysis (alteplase) is reserved for massive/life-threatening PE with haemodynamic compromise. Option E is INCORRECT — surgical embolectomy is only for massive PE refractory to thrombolysis.

label: Lobar Consolidation
stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of productive cough, fever (38.8°C), and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. His SpO₂ is 91% on air. The CXR shows right lower lobe homogeneous opacification with air bronchograms. His CURB-65 score is 3. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Hospital admission, IV co-amoxiclav plus IV clarithromycin
Oral amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days and discharge
CT chest to exclude lung cancer before antibiotics
Oral doxycycline 200 mg once daily for 5 days
IV piperacillin-tazobactam empirically
correctAnswerText: Hospital admission, IV co-amoxiclav plus IV clarithromycin
explanation: The CXR shows right lower lobe consolidation (homogeneous opacity, air bronchograms) consistent with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP). CURB-65 score of 3 indicates severe CAP with high mortality risk (15–40%). Option A is CORRECT. NG138 recommends hospital admission for CURB-65 ≥3. Dual antibiotic therapy combining IV beta-lactam (co-amoxiclav or amoxicillin) with IV macrolide (clarithromycin) provides coverage for typical and atypical pathogens. SpO₂ 91% requires supplemental oxygen. Option B is INCORRECT — oral amoxicillin alone is appropriate only for low-severity CAP (CURB-65 0–1) managed in the community. Option C is INCORRECT — cancer investigation should be arranged after treating the acute infection; a 6-week follow-up CXR is standard. Option D is INCORRECT — doxycycline is suitable for mild-moderate CAP but not severe hospital-requiring pneumonia. Option E is INCORRECT — piperacillin-tazobactam covers gram-negative/Pseudomonas organisms and is not standard empirical therapy for CAP unless hospital-acquired or aspiration.

label: Tension Pneumothorax
stem: A 30-year-old man is brought in after a road traffic accident. He is increasingly distressed, HR 130 bpm, BP 80/50 mmHg, tracheal deviation to the right, absent breath sounds on the left and distended neck veins. The CXR is shown. What is the immediate management?
options: Immediate needle decompression — 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line on the left
Insert chest drain on the left side immediately
Arrange urgent CT thorax
IV fluid resuscitation with 2 L normal saline
Endotracheal intubation and ventilation first
correctAnswerText: Immediate needle decompression — 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line on the left
explanation:
This is a tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate treatment before imaging. Signs: haemodynamic collapse, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, absent breath sounds, distended neck veins. The CXR confirms left-sided tension pneumothorax with tracheal shift.

Option A is CORRECT. Tension pneumothorax is immediately life-threatening. Treatment must not be delayed for imaging. Emergency needle decompression using a 14G cannula at the 2nd ICS, mid-clavicular line on the affected (left) side converts tension to simple pneumothorax, followed immediately by a formal chest drain.

Option B is INCORRECT — while a chest drain is the definitive treatment, needle decompression is faster and buys time in extremis.

Option C is INCORRECT — CT before treatment would cause death. Clinical diagnosis is sufficient.

Option D is INCORRECT — IV fluids do not treat the underlying cause. They may briefly sustain BP but tension pneumothorax requires decompression.

Option E is INCORRECT — intubation and positive pressure ventilation in tension pneumothorax will worsen haemodynamic collapse until decompression is performed.

label: Finger Clubbing
stem: A 52-year-old man with a 40-pack-year history is referred with a chronic productive cough and weight loss. On examination, the image shown is noted on both hands. Which condition is the most common cause of this finding in the UK?
options: Bronchogenic carcinoma
Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
Cystic fibrosis
Congenital heart disease
Mesothelioma
correctAnswerText: Bronchogenic carcinoma
explanation:
The image shows finger clubbing — loss of the normal angle at the nail base (Lovibond angle >180°), increased nail bed fluctuation, and bulbous fingertip enlargement.

Option A is CORRECT. In adults in the UK, bronchogenic carcinoma (especially squamous cell and adenocarcinoma) is the single most common cause of acquired digital clubbing. The strong smoking history and weight loss make lung cancer highly likely here. Urgent chest imaging and bronchoscopy/CT are required.

Option B is INCORRECT — IPF does cause clubbing (~50% of cases) but is less common than lung cancer overall.

Option C is INCORRECT — cystic fibrosis causes clubbing but typically presents in younger patients.

Option D is INCORRECT — congenital heart disease (particularly cyanotic) causes clubbing but this is a congenital rather than acquired cause in adults.

Option E is INCORRECT — mesothelioma can cause clubbing but is less common than primary bronchogenic carcinoma.

label: Pancoast Horner
stem: A 59-year-old male smoker presents with right shoulder pain radiating down the medial arm for 3 months. On examination, there is right-sided ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis. His CXR shows an opacity at the right lung apex. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pancoast tumour with Horner's syndrome
Cervical disc herniation at C7–T1
Brachial neuritis (Neuralgic amyotrophy)
Right-sided apical pneumonia
Subclavian artery aneurysm
correctAnswerText: Pancoast tumour with Horner's syndrome
explanation:
The combination of apical lung mass, ipsilateral shoulder/arm pain (T1 nerve root), and Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis — sympathetic chain involvement) is pathognomonic for a Pancoast tumour.

Option A is CORRECT. A Pancoast tumour (superior sulcus tumour) is most commonly non-small cell lung cancer (squamous cell or adenocarcinoma) at the lung apex. It invades adjacent structures: lower brachial plexus (T1 — medial arm pain/weakness), sympathetic chain (Horner's syndrome), subclavian vessels, and ribs. It requires CT/PET staging and MRI for local invasion assessment.

Option B is INCORRECT — cervical disc prolapse at C7–T1 causes radiculopathy but does not cause Horner's syndrome or an apical lung mass.

Option C is INCORRECT — neuralgic amyotrophy causes acute brachial plexus inflammation; it is self-limiting and does not cause an apical mass.

Option D is INCORRECT — apical pneumonia typically presents with acute fever, does not cause Horner's syndrome, and resolves.

Option E is INCORRECT — subclavian aneurysm may cause brachial symptoms but not Horner's + apical mass.

label: Bells Palsy
stem: A 38-year-old woman wakes with sudden right-sided facial weakness. She cannot close her right eye, her right forehead does not wrinkle on raising her eyebrows, and the right side of her mouth droops. There is no limb weakness, dysarthria, or dysphagia. She reported pain behind her right ear 2 days ago. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Prednisolone 50 mg once daily for 10 days, started within 72 hours
Urgent CT head and neurology referral
IV aciclovir for Ramsay Hunt syndrome
Physiotherapy and eye patch only — no medication needed
MRI brain to exclude cerebellopontine angle tumour
correctAnswerText: Prednisolone 50 mg once daily for 10 days, started within 72 hours
explanation: This is Bell's palsy — acute lower motor neurone (LMN) facial nerve palsy affecting all three divisions (forehead, eye, mouth). LMN lesion affects forehead (frontalis) unlike upper motor neurone lesions where forehead is spared. Option A is CORRECT. Oral prednisolone 50 mg OD for 10 days is the evidence-based treatment for Bell's palsy if started within 72 hours of onset. This improves rates of complete recovery. Eye protection (lubricating drops, tape at night) is essential to prevent corneal exposure. CKS recommends against routine antiviral monotherapy but some guidelines add aciclovir for severe cases. Option B is INCORRECT — CT head is not required for a straightforward LMN palsy with no other neurological signs. The peripheral nature is confirmed by forehead involvement. Option C is INCORRECT — Ramsay Hunt syndrome (varicella zoster reactivation) presents with vesicles in the ear/mouth. This patient has no vesicles, so antiviral addition is optional, not first-line. Option D is INCORRECT — without steroids, ~30% have incomplete recovery with synkinesis. Option E is INCORRECT — MRI is only indicated if there is atypical presentation (gradual onset, other cranial nerve involvement, parotid mass).

label: Chlamydial Conjunctivitis
stem: A 22-year-old sexually active man presents with a 3-week history of bilateral mucopurulent eye discharge and mild red eyes. He also reports urethral discharge. His eyes show follicular conjunctivitis. Bacterial swabs for gonorrhoea are negative. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days plus azithromycin 1 g single dose
Topical chloramphenicol eye drops only
IV ceftriaxone 500 mg single dose
Oral aciclovir for herpetic conjunctivitis
Topical fusidic acid and reassurance
correctAnswerText: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days plus azithromycin 1 g single dose
explanation:
The clinical picture — chronic mucopurulent conjunctivitis in a sexually active young man with follicular reaction and concurrent urethral discharge — is highly suggestive of chlamydial conjunctivitis (Chlamydia trachomatis, serotypes D–K).

Option A is CORRECT. Adult inclusion conjunctivitis from Chlamydia trachomatis requires systemic antibiotics as topical treatment alone is insufficient (concurrent genital infection). BASHH guidelines recommend oral doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g single dose). Both partners must be treated and contact tracing performed.

Option B is INCORRECT — topical antibiotics do not treat the genital reservoir of chlamydial infection.

Option C is INCORRECT — IV ceftriaxone treats gonorrhoea (excluded by negative swabs).

Option D is INCORRECT — viral (herpetic) conjunctivitis presents acutely with vesicular lesions; chronic follicular conjunctivitis in an STI context is chlamydial.

Option E is INCORRECT — fusidic acid treats staphylococcal conjunctivitis, not chlamydia.

label: Hypertensive Retinopathy
stem: A 58-year-old man with known hypertension presents for a routine review. His BP is 178/112 mmHg. Fundoscopy reveals arteriovenous nipping, copper wiring, flame-shaped haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, and blurring of the optic disc margins. How should this finding be classified?
options: Grade IV hypertensive retinopathy — requires same-day emergency referral
Grade II hypertensive retinopathy — routine hypertension management
Grade III hypertensive retinopathy — urgent (within 24h) BP control
Diabetic maculopathy — arrange HbA1c
Normal fundoscopic findings for age
correctAnswerText: Grade IV hypertensive retinopathy — requires same-day emergency referral
explanation:
Hypertensive retinopathy is graded by the Keith-Wagener-Barker classification. The findings here include cotton-wool spots, haemorrhages, AND papilloedema (blurring of optic disc margins) — this is Grade IV.

Option A is CORRECT. Grade IV hypertensive retinopathy includes papilloedema, indicating raised intracranial pressure secondary to malignant/accelerated hypertension. This represents a hypertensive emergency requiring same-day hospital admission for controlled BP reduction (not too fast — risk of ischaemic stroke). Target: reduce MAP by no more than 25% in first hour.

Option B is INCORRECT — Grade II shows AV nipping and arterial changes only; no haemorrhages or exudates.

Option C is INCORRECT — Grade III has haemorrhages and exudates without papilloedema; needs urgent referral but not a hypertensive emergency.

Option D is INCORRECT — diabetic maculopathy shows hard exudates in a macular distribution, microaneurysms and neovascularisation; no AV nipping.

Option E is INCORRECT — these are all abnormal findings indicating target organ damage.

label: Meningococcal Septicaemia
stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his parents. He has had a fever and vomiting for 6 hours and is now drowsy. On examination, a widespread non-blanching purpuric rash is found across the trunk and legs. The image shown is from his right leg. What is the immediate management?
options: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg immediately, before transfer to PICU
Take blood cultures first and await results before antibiotics
Lumbar puncture to confirm meningococcal disease
IV aciclovir for viral encephalitis
Observe for 4 hours and reassess
correctAnswerText: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg immediately, before transfer to PICU
explanation: The non-blanching purpuric rash in a febrile, unwell child is meningococcal septicaemia (Neisseria meningitidis) until proven otherwise. This is a life-threatening medical emergency. Option A is CORRECT. CKS and NG51 mandate immediate IV or IM benzylpenicillin (or IV ceftriaxone if available) WITHOUT waiting for investigations. Antibiotics must not be delayed for blood cultures or LP in a critically ill child. Transfer to PICU/HDU is required urgently. Option B is INCORRECT — NEVER delay antibiotics waiting for blood cultures in suspected meningococcal disease. Mortality risk is very high without immediate treatment. Option C is INCORRECT — lumbar puncture is contraindicated in a haemodynamically unstable patient with a coagulopathy/petechial rash until stabilised. Option D is INCORRECT — aciclovir would be considered for encephalitis (herpes), not septicaemia with purpuric rash. Option E is INCORRECT — a child with non-blanching purpura and deteriorating consciousness requires immediate treatment, not watchful waiting.

label: Chickenpox
stem: A 6-year-old girl presents with a 2-day history of fever and a widespread vesicular rash. The image shows lesions in different stages — macules, papules, vesicles, and crusted lesions — distributed across the trunk and face. She attends primary school. What is the most appropriate advice to her parents?
options: She is infectious until all lesions are crusted — keep her off school for at least 5 days from rash onset or until all blisters have crusted
She may return to school immediately if she feels well
Start oral aciclovir immediately in all cases
She needs VZV immunoglobulin (VZIG) urgently
Refer to paediatrics for IV antibiotics for secondary bacterial infection
correctAnswerText: She is infectious until all lesions are crusted — keep her off school for at least 5 days from rash onset or until all blisters have crusted
explanation:
The characteristic rash — lesions in multiple stages simultaneously (macules, papules, vesicles, crusts), centripetal distribution, pruritic — is diagnostic of primary varicella (chickenpox), caused by Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV).

Option A is CORRECT. Children with chickenpox are infectious from 1–2 days before the rash appears until all lesions are crusted (typically 5 days after rash onset). PHE guidance advises exclusion from school for at least 5 days from rash onset and until all blisters are crusted. Treatment is supportive (calamine lotion, antihistamines for itch, paracetamol for fever — NOT ibuprofen which may increase risk of invasive Group A Strep).

Option B is INCORRECT — children are still highly infectious during the blistering phase.

Option C is INCORRECT — oral aciclovir is only recommended in immunocompromised individuals, adults (where disease is more severe), and neonates.

Option D is INCORRECT — VZIG is given to susceptible contacts who are at risk (immunocompromised, neonates, pregnant women) to prevent disease. This patient already has chickenpox.

Option E is INCORRECT — uncomplicated chickenpox does not require antibiotics.

label: Erythema Infectiosum
stem: A 7-year-old child presents with a bright red facial rash on both cheeks giving a "slapped" appearance, followed by a lacy reticular rash on the arms. She has been mildly febrile but is now apyrexial and well. Her mother is 24 weeks pregnant. What is the most important concern?
options: Parvovirus B19 infection — urgent referral of the pregnant mother to obstetrics
Rubella — admit the child for isolation
Scarlet fever — start oral phenoxymethylpenicillin
Roseola infantum — reassure mother
Kawasaki disease — check for coronary artery aneurysm
correctAnswerText: Parvovirus B19 infection — urgent referral of the pregnant mother to obstetrics
explanation:
The clinical picture — bilateral bright red facial rash ("slapped cheeks") followed by a lacy reticular rash on the limbs, in an otherwise well child — is classic of erythema infectiosum (Fifth disease), caused by Parvovirus B19.

Option A is CORRECT. While erythema infectiosum is benign and self-limiting in healthy children, Parvovirus B19 in pregnancy can cause hydrops fetalis (severe foetal anaemia) especially before 20 weeks gestation. A 24-week-pregnant mother exposed to parvovirus B19 requires urgent obstetric referral for maternal serology (IgM/IgG) and foetal surveillance by ultrasound.

Option B is INCORRECT — rubella causes a different rash (pink maculopapular, starting on face, spreading caudally) with postauricular lymphadenopathy.

Option C is INCORRECT — scarlet fever has a sandpaper-textured diffuse rash with circumoral pallor and strawberry tongue, not isolated facial erythema.

Option D is INCORRECT — roseola (HHV-6) causes high fever then rash on the trunk after fever resolves — different pattern.

Option E is INCORRECT — Kawasaki requires 5+ days of fever plus 4 other criteria.

label: Scarlet Fever
stem: A 9-year-old boy presents with a 2-day history of sore throat, fever, and a diffuse rash. The rash has the texture of sandpaper and is more prominent in the skin folds. Examination reveals a red strawberry tongue and circumoral pallor. What is the correct treatment?
options: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) 500 mg four times daily for 10 days
Oral amoxicillin — first-line for streptococcal infection
Topical hydrocortisone cream for the rash
No treatment needed — self-limiting viral illness
IV benzylpenicillin — oral antibiotics are insufficient
correctAnswerText: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) 500 mg four times daily for 10 days
explanation: The combination of sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, circumoral pallor, and skin fold accentuation (Pastia's lines) with sore throat and fever is diagnostic of scarlet fever caused by Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes). Option A is CORRECT. CKS recommends oral phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) 500 mg QDS for 10 days as first-line treatment. This reduces risk of complications (rheumatic fever, post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis) and shortens illness. Erythromycin or clarithromycin if penicillin allergic. Scarlet fever is a notifiable disease — inform the local Health Protection Team. Option B is INCORRECT — amoxicillin should be AVOIDED in suspected streptococcal infection as it can cause a widespread maculopapular rash if EBV infection (infectious mononucleosis) is co-present or misdiagnosed. Option C is INCORRECT — topical steroids treat inflammation, not the underlying Group A Strep infection. Option D is INCORRECT — untreated scarlet fever risks rheumatic fever and post-streptococcal GN. Option E is INCORRECT — oral penicillin V has excellent bioavailability and is effective for uncomplicated scarlet fever.

label: Psoriasis
stem: A 34-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of itchy, scaly skin lesions predominantly on the elbows, knees, and scalp. The image shows well-demarcated plaques with a silvery scale on the trunk. He also mentions joint pain in his fingers. What is the first-line topical treatment?
options: Potent topical corticosteroid (e.g. betamethasone valerate 0.1%) combined with topical vitamin D analogue (calcipotriol)
Emollients only
Topical coal tar shampoo only
Systemic methotrexate
Topical antifungal cream
correctAnswerText: Potent topical corticosteroid (e.g. betamethasone valerate 0.1%) combined with topical vitamin D analogue (calcipotriol)
explanation: The well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale are characteristic of chronic plaque psoriasis. The joint pain in fingers raises concern for psoriatic arthropathy (which occurs in ~30% of psoriasis patients). Option A is CORRECT. NG153 recommends a potent corticosteroid combined with a vitamin D analogue (e.g. betamethasone + calcipotriol — available as Dovobet/Enstilar) as first-line topical treatment for plaque psoriasis on the body. This combination is more effective than either agent alone. Option B is INCORRECT — emollients are an adjunct to reduce scale and itch, not definitive treatment. Option C is INCORRECT — coal tar is an alternative topical option but is messy, smelly, and less effective than combination steroid/VitD. Option D is INCORRECT — systemic methotrexate is reserved for moderate-severe psoriasis affecting >10% BSA or inadequately controlled with topicals. Option E is INCORRECT — psoriasis is not fungal; antifungals treat tinea, not psoriasis.

label: Malignant Melanoma
stem: A 52-year-old fair-skinned woman presents with a pigmented lesion on her back that her partner noticed has changed over the past few months. The image shows an asymmetric lesion with irregular borders, multiple colours (brown, black, pink), and a diameter greater than 6 mm. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Urgent 2-week-wait referral to dermatology for suspected melanoma
Reassure and review in 6 months
Prescribe a topical antifungal in case this is tinea nigra
Shave biopsy in primary care
Cryotherapy to the lesion
correctAnswerText: Urgent 2-week-wait referral to dermatology for suspected melanoma
explanation: The ABCDE criteria for melanoma are met here: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation (multiple colours), Diameter >6 mm, and Evolution. This is strongly suspicious for malignant melanoma. Option A is CORRECT. NG12 mandates urgent 2-week-wait (2WW) referral to a specialist for any pigmented lesion meeting melanoma criteria. The 2WW pathway ensures the patient is seen within 2 weeks by a dermatologist for dermoscopy-guided excision biopsy. Option B is INCORRECT — reassurance is inappropriate for a lesion meeting multiple ABCDE criteria. Delay increases mortality. Option C is INCORRECT — tinea nigra is rare and presents as flat, non-raised brown patch on palms; not a differential for this lesion. Option D is INCORRECT — shave biopsy is contraindicated for melanoma as it may provide incomplete histological staging (Breslow thickness inaccurate). Option E is INCORRECT — cryotherapy destroys tissue and precludes histological diagnosis. Never treat an undiagnosed pigmented lesion.

label: Basal Cell Carcinoma
stem: A 68-year-old fair-skinned man with a 40-year history of outdoor work presents with a slowly growing lesion on his left cheek. The image shows a pearly, skin-coloured nodule with rolled (everted) borders and telangiectasia. It has been growing for 2 years. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Urgent referral to dermatology for surgical excision with clear margins
Urgent 2-week-wait referral as for suspected melanoma
Reassure — basal cell carcinoma never metastasises so monitoring is sufficient
Prescribe topical imiquimod for all BCCs
Cryotherapy in primary care
correctAnswerText: Urgent referral to dermatology for surgical excision with clear margins
explanation: The pearly nodule with rolled borders, telangiectasia, and slow growth on a sun-exposed site in an elderly man is classic nodular basal cell carcinoma (BCC) — the most common skin cancer in the UK. Option A is CORRECT. recommends urgent dermatology referral for confirmed or suspected BCC. Surgical excision with 3–5 mm clear margins is the standard treatment for nodular BCC on the face. While BCC very rarely metastasises, it causes significant local destruction if untreated (rodent ulcer). Option B is INCORRECT — 2WW referral is for suspected squamous cell carcinoma or melanoma (higher metastatic risk). BCC is referred urgently but does not meet the same 2WW criteria. Option C is INCORRECT — though metastasis is rare (<0.5%), untreated BCC causes extensive local tissue destruction, particularly on the face. Option D is INCORRECT — topical imiquimod (5% cream) is appropriate for superficial BCC only, not nodular BCC on the face. Option E is INCORRECT — cryotherapy has lower cure rates for facial BCC and does not provide histological confirmation of clear margins.

label: Atopic Eczema
stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought by his mother with a 12-month history of intensely itchy, dry, inflamed skin in the antecubital fossae and popliteal fossae. The image shows lichenified, excoriated patches with weeping. He has a history of asthma and his mother has hay fever. What is the correct stepwise approach?
options: Emollients as base treatment plus mild topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%) for flares
Wet wraps and oral antihistamines only
Start systemic ciclosporin immediately
Topical tacrolimus (Protopic) as first-line treatment
Oral prednisolone for 5 days
correctAnswerText: Emollients as base treatment plus mild topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%) for flares
explanation: The pruritic, flexural, lichenified rash with personal and family atopic history is diagnostic of atopic eczema. The atopic triad (eczema + asthma + hay fever) is present. Option A is CORRECT. NG190 recommends a stepwise approach: emollients (first-line, applied liberally, multiple daily applications) as the foundation, with mild topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1%) for flares on the face and flexures in children. Twice-daily application during flares; reduce to lowest effective strength. Option B is INCORRECT — wet wraps are appropriate for severe flares as an add-on, not primary treatment. Oral antihistamines have limited evidence in eczema. Option C is INCORRECT — ciclosporin is reserved for severe, refractory atopic eczema in secondary care. Option D is INCORRECT — tacrolimus (a calcineurin inhibitor) is second-line when steroids are inappropriate (face, eyelids, skin folds with prolonged use concerns), not first-line. Option E is INCORRECT — oral prednisolone is not recommended for eczema in children; tachyphylaxis and rebound are significant concerns.

label: Herpes Zoster Shingles
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of severe burning pain on the right side of her chest, followed by a vesicular rash in a unilateral dermatomal distribution that does not cross the midline. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily for 7 days, started within 72 hours
Topical aciclovir cream applied to lesions
Oral prednisolone to prevent post-herpetic neuralgia
No antiviral treatment — self-limiting condition
IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg three times daily
correctAnswerText: Oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily for 7 days, started within 72 hours
explanation: Unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash preceded by pain is diagnostic of herpes zoster (shingles) — reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus in the dorsal root ganglia. Option A is CORRECT. CKS recommends oral aciclovir 800 mg 5 times daily for 7 days for uncomplicated shingles, started within 72 hours of rash onset (most effective if started early). This reduces severity, duration, and risk of post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN). Elderly patients (>50) particularly benefit. Amitriptyline or gabapentin for post-herpetic neuralgia if it occurs. Option B is INCORRECT — topical aciclovir cream does not achieve sufficient tissue levels for systemic VZV reactivation. Option C is INCORRECT — oral corticosteroids do not prevent post-herpetic neuralgia and may cause immunosuppression. Option D is INCORRECT — untreated shingles leads to prolonged illness and higher risk of PHN, particularly in the elderly. Option E is INCORRECT — IV aciclovir is reserved for immunocompromised patients, ophthalmic zoster, or Ramsay Hunt syndrome.

label: Urticaria
stem: A 28-year-old woman develops sudden onset intensely itchy raised wheals covering her trunk and arms 30 minutes after eating prawns at a restaurant. She has no stridor, no lip or tongue swelling, BP 120/80 mmHg, and SpO₂ 99%. What is the first-line treatment?
options: Oral cetirizine 10 mg (or chlorphenamine 4 mg) and observe
Intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000)
IV hydrocortisone 200 mg immediately
Discharge with antihistamine prescription only
Oral prednisolone 40 mg for 5 days
correctAnswerText: Oral cetirizine 10 mg (or chlorphenamine 4 mg) and observe
explanation:
The raised, itchy wheals (urticaria) after shellfish ingestion in a patient with normal vitals and no anaphylaxis signs (no angioedema of tongue/lips, no stridor, no hypotension) is acute urticaria — a type I hypersensitivity reaction.

Option A is CORRECT. For urticaria without systemic features of anaphylaxis, a non-sedating antihistamine (cetirizine 10 mg) or sedating antihistamine (chlorphenamine 4 mg) is first-line. The patient should be observed for 4–6 hours before discharge given the history of possible allergen exposure.

Option B is INCORRECT — IM adrenaline is the treatment for anaphylaxis (hypotension, stridor, angioedema, bronchospasm). This patient is haemodynamically stable with isolated urticaria.

Option C is INCORRECT — IV hydrocortisone is an adjunct in anaphylaxis, not first-line for isolated urticaria.

Option D is INCORRECT — immediate discharge without observation is unsafe given the allergen trigger and potential for delayed or biphasic reaction.

Option E is INCORRECT — oral prednisolone is sometimes used for severe urticaria but is second-line after antihistamines.

label: Scabies
stem: A 22-year-old university student presents with a 4-week history of intense nocturnal itch affecting his hands, wrists, and genital area. His flatmate has similar symptoms. The image shows linear burrows between the fingers. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Permethrin 5% cream — applied to entire body from neck down, left on for 8–12 hours
Topical hydrocortisone cream for 2 weeks
Oral antihistamine only for symptom relief
Malathion 0.5% lotion — applied only to affected areas
Systemic ivermectin only — topical treatments are ineffective
correctAnswerText: Permethrin 5% cream — applied to entire body from neck down, left on for 8–12 hours
explanation: Linear burrows (pathognomonic of Sarcoptes scabiei mite tunnelling), nocturnal itch, interdigital and genital distribution, and household contact with similar symptoms confirms scabies. Option A is CORRECT. Permethrin 5% cream is the recommended first-line treatment in the UK (CKS). It is applied to the entire body surface from the neck downwards (including under fingernails, genitalia, skin folds), left for 8–12 hours, then washed off. A repeat application after 7 days is recommended. All household contacts must be treated simultaneously. Wash all bedding and clothing at 60°C. Option B is INCORRECT — topical steroids treat itch but not the underlying mite infestation. Option C is INCORRECT — antihistamines address itch (antihistamine-insensitive in scabies) but do not eradicate the mite. Option D is INCORRECT — malathion is second-line (first-line in pregnancy as permethrin avoided). It should also be applied to the whole body, not just affected areas. Option E is INCORRECT — oral ivermectin (unlicensed in UK) is used for crusted (Norwegian) scabies or institutional outbreaks, not routine scabies.

label: Pityriasis Rosea
stem: A 19-year-old woman presents with a widespread rash on her trunk that developed over 2 weeks. She noticed a single larger oval scaly patch on her chest 2 weeks before the rash spread. The image shows multiple oval salmon-pink plaques arranged in a "Christmas tree" pattern along the lines of cleavage on the back. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassurance and emollients — self-limiting condition resolving in 6–8 weeks
Oral aciclovir — caused by HHV-6/7 reactivation
Oral terbinafine for tinea versicolor
Urgent dermatology referral for secondary syphilis exclusion
Potent topical corticosteroids for 4 weeks
correctAnswerText: Reassurance and emollients — self-limiting condition resolving in 6–8 weeks
explanation:
The herald patch followed by a Christmas tree distribution of oval scaly plaques along Langer's cleavage lines is diagnostic of pityriasis rosea, a self-limiting condition likely caused by HHV-6/7 reactivation.

Option A is CORRECT. Pityriasis rosea is benign and self-limiting, resolving in 6–8 weeks without treatment. Management is reassurance plus emollients to soothe dryness and itch, and antihistamines for pruritus if needed. Sunlight (UVB) may accelerate resolution.

Option B is INCORRECT — while HHV-6/7 is implicated, antiviral treatment is not recommended routinely as the evidence is limited.

Option C is INCORRECT — tinea versicolor presents differently (hypopigmented/hyperpigmented macules, fine scale, positive Wood's lamp). No herald patch.

Option D is INCORRECT — secondary syphilis can mimic pityriasis rosea (including palm/sole involvement) so RPR/VDRL should be checked if clinically uncertain, but this presentation is classic PR. Urgent referral is not required.

Option E is INCORRECT — potent topical steroids are not standard treatment for pityriasis rosea and prolonged use carries risks.

label: Tinea Corporis
stem: A 25-year-old man who plays contact sports presents with a circular, itchy, scaly rash on his forearm. The image shows an annular plaque with a raised, scaly border and central clearing. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Topical terbinafine 1% cream once daily for 1–2 weeks
Topical hydrocortisone 1% — eczema is the diagnosis
Oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose
Topical fusidic acid for bacterial infection
Oral griseofulvin for 6 months
correctAnswerText: Topical terbinafine 1% cream once daily for 1–2 weeks
explanation:
The annular plaque with raised scaly advancing border and central clearing is the hallmark of tinea corporis (ringworm), a dermatophyte fungal infection. Contact sports are a common transmission route.

Option A is CORRECT. Topical terbinafine 1% cream (once daily for 1–2 weeks) is first-line for localised tinea corporis, with excellent cure rates. Clotrimazole or miconazole are alternatives. Confirm diagnosis with skin scraping for mycology if uncertain.

Option B is INCORRECT — topical steroids worsen tinea fungal infections by suppressing the immune response, creating 'tinea incognito' (masked, atypical presentation). Topical steroid-antifungal combinations (e.g. Canesten HC) are sometimes used for initial inflammation but pure steroid is inappropriate.

Option C is INCORRECT — oral fluconazole is used for tinea capitis or extensive tinea corporis, not a single small patch.

Option D is INCORRECT — fusidic acid treats staphylococcal skin infections; dermatophytes are fungi.

Option E is INCORRECT — oral griseofulvin is reserved for tinea capitis (scalp) and onychomycosis.

label: Seborrhoeic Dermatitis
stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of greasy, scaly patches on his scalp, nasolabial folds, eyebrows, and chest. The patches are yellowish with mild erythema. He reports the condition flares with stress. What is the most likely causative organism?
options: Malassezia furfur (formerly Pityrosporum ovale)
Staphylococcus aureus
Candida albicans
Herpes simplex virus
No organism — this is an autoimmune condition
correctAnswerText: Malassezia furfur (formerly Pityrosporum ovale)
explanation:
Seborrhoeic dermatitis presents with greasy yellowish scales in sebum-rich areas (scalp, nasolabial folds, eyebrows, central chest, ears) with mild erythema. The pathogenesis involves the yeast Malassezia furfur (formerly Pityrosporum ovale) causing an inflammatory response.

Option A is CORRECT. Malassezia furfur colonises sebum-rich skin and triggers an inflammatory response in susceptible individuals (possibly related to an abnormal immune reaction to the yeast). This is why ketoconazole (antifungal) shampoo and cream are effective treatments. Stress worsens seborrhoeic dermatitis by altering skin immunity.

Option B is INCORRECT — Staphylococcus aureus causes impetigo and infected eczema, not seborrhoeic dermatitis.

Option C is INCORRECT — Candida causes intertrigo (skin folds, moist areas) with satellite lesions, not scalp/face dermatitis.

Option D is INCORRECT — HSV causes cold sores and genital herpes; not implicated in seborrhoeic dermatitis.

Option E is INCORRECT — this is a yeast-driven inflammatory condition, not primarily autoimmune.

label: Herpes Zoster Dermatomal
stem: A 65-year-old man presents with severe burning pain in a band across the left side of his abdomen followed 3 days later by a vesicular eruption in the same distribution, not crossing the midline. He is otherwise well and immunocompetent. He is seen on day 5 of the rash. Should antiviral therapy be started?
options: No — antiviral treatment is most effective within 72 hours; at day 5 with mild disease it offers minimal benefit. Focus on pain management.
Yes — start IV aciclovir immediately
Yes — start oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily regardless of timing
No — antivirals are only for immunocompromised patients
Yes — start oral prednisolone to reduce post-herpetic neuralgia
correctAnswerText: No — antiviral treatment is most effective within 72 hours; at day 5 with mild disease it offers minimal benefit. Focus on pain management.
explanation:
This is herpes zoster (shingles) — unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash following a prodrome of pain. The dermatomal distribution not crossing the midline is pathognomonic.

Option A is CORRECT. Antiviral therapy (aciclovir, valaciclovir, famciclovir) is most effective when started within 72 hours of rash onset. After 72 hours in an otherwise well, immunocompetent patient with mild-moderate disease, the benefit is minimal. Focus should shift to pain management: paracetamol, NSAIDs, and if needed, amitriptyline or gabapentin for neuropathic pain (post-herpetic neuralgia prevention).

Option B is INCORRECT — IV aciclovir is reserved for immunocompromised patients, disseminated disease, ophthalmic or Ramsay Hunt zoster.

Option C is INCORRECT — starting oral antivirals after day 5 in mild uncomplicated zoster in an immunocompetent adult is not recommended.

Option D is INCORRECT — antivirals ARE recommended in immunocompromised patients regardless of timing.

Option E is INCORRECT — oral steroids do not prevent post-herpetic neuralgia and may cause immunosuppression.

label: Erythema Nodosum
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with painful, tender, raised red nodules on both shins. She also has a dry cough, fever, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on CXR. What is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
options: Sarcoidosis
Streptococcal throat infection
Tuberculosis
Crohn's disease
Behçet's disease
correctAnswerText: Sarcoidosis
explanation:
Erythema nodosum (EN) presents as painful, raised, erythematous nodules on the anterior shins — a form of panniculitis (inflammation of subcutaneous fat). The combination of EN + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy + dry cough + fever in a young woman is classic Löfgren's syndrome — an acute presentation of sarcoidosis.

Option A is CORRECT. Löfgren's syndrome comprises: erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy ± ankle arthritis ± fever, and carries an excellent prognosis with spontaneous resolution in >80% of cases. It is the commonest presentation of acute sarcoidosis.

Option B is INCORRECT — streptococcal infection is a common cause of EN but does not explain bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy.

Option C is INCORRECT — TB causes EN and hilar lymphadenopathy but is less likely than sarcoidosis in a young woman in the UK without TB risk factors.

Option D is INCORRECT — Crohn's disease causes EN but typically presents with GI symptoms, and does not cause hilar lymphadenopathy.

Option E is INCORRECT — Behçet's disease causes EN plus oral/genital ulcers and uveitis; it is rare in the UK.

label: Cellulitis
stem: A 55-year-old obese man with type 2 diabetes presents with a 3-day history of increasing redness, warmth, swelling, and pain over his right lower leg. The image shows a well-demarcated area of erythema and oedema. Temperature is 38.2°C, WCC 14 × 10⁹/L. His Eron classification score is Class II. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5–7 days
IV benzylpenicillin immediately — oral antibiotics are inadequate
Topical mupirocin cream applied to the affected area
Oral co-amoxiclav to cover MRSA
Intravenous vancomycin empirically
correctAnswerText: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5–7 days
