explanation: Cellulitis presents as warm, erythematous, tender, oedematous skin, typically unilateral on the leg. Causative organisms are usually Streptococcus pyogenes and Staphylococcus aureus. Eron Class II = systemic signs (fever, raised WCC) but tolerating oral antibiotics. Option A is CORRECT. For Eron Class II cellulitis, oral antibiotics are appropriate if the patient can tolerate oral treatment and absorption is reliable. Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS (500–1000 mg in severe cases) for 5–7 days covers both Staph and Strep. CKS recommends co-amoxiclav if there is a penetrating injury/immunocompromise/gram-negative organism concern, but flucloxacillin covers standard cellulitis. Option B is INCORRECT — IV antibiotics are indicated for Eron Class III/IV (hypotension, rapid spread, necrotising features) or when oral route is not tolerated. Option C is INCORRECT — topical antibiotics cannot penetrate to the dermis where the infection is located. Option D is INCORRECT — co-amoxiclav is not specifically needed for MRSA coverage. MRSA cellulitis is relatively rare and requires specific cover (doxycycline, trimethoprim, or vancomycin). Option E is INCORRECT — vancomycin is for confirmed/suspected MRSA; empirical use is inappropriate.

label: Acne Vulgaris
stem: A 17-year-old girl presents with moderate acne involving the face and upper back. The image shows open and closed comedones, inflammatory papules, pustules, and a few nodules. She has tried over-the-counter benzoyl peroxide for 8 weeks with inadequate response. What is the next most appropriate treatment?
options: Topical adapalene (retinoid) plus topical antibiotic (clindamycin) gel
Oral tetracycline monotherapy
Oral isotretinoin (Roaccutane)
Topical ketoconazole shampoo
Intralesional triamcinolone injections to all pustules
correctAnswerText: Topical adapalene (retinoid) plus topical antibiotic (clindamycin) gel
explanation: Moderate acne (comedones + inflammatory papules/pustules + nodules) failing OTC treatment requires step-up therapy. Option A is CORRECT. NG198 recommends topical retinoid (adapalene 0.1%) combined with a topical antibiotic (clindamycin) as the preferred regimen for moderate acne after OTC failure. This combination addresses both comedonal and inflammatory components. Benzoyl peroxide should be co-prescribed or alternated to reduce antibiotic resistance. Option B is INCORRECT — oral tetracyclines (doxycycline, lymecycline) are indicated for moderate-severe acne with widespread involvement or inadequate topical response, and should generally be combined with a topical retinoid, not used alone. Option C is INCORRECT — oral isotretinoin is reserved for severe nodular/cystic acne or moderate acne failing two courses of antibiotics plus topicals. Requires shared-care prescribing, teratogenicity counselling and contraception (CPPE/iPLEDGE equivalent). Option D is INCORRECT — ketoconazole treats Malassezia folliculitis (fungal), not acne vulgaris. Option E is INCORRECT — intralesional steroids are occasionally used for individual cystic nodules, not widespread moderate acne.

label: Rosacea
stem: A 45-year-old fair-skinned woman presents with persistent central facial redness, flushing, telangiectasia, and papulopustular eruptions on the nose and cheeks. The image shows the typical distribution. She reports worsening with alcohol, hot drinks, and sun exposure. What triggers should she avoid AND what is first-line topical treatment?
options: Avoid alcohol, hot drinks, spicy food, sun exposure — first-line: topical metronidazole 0.75% gel
Avoid all fatty foods — first-line: topical adapalene retinoid gel
Avoid stress only — first-line: topical hydrocortisone cream
No trigger avoidance needed — oral antibiotics first-line for all rosacea
Avoid gluten — first-line: topical antifungal cream
correctAnswerText: Avoid alcohol, hot drinks, spicy food, sun exposure — first-line: topical metronidazole 0.75% gel
explanation: Rosacea presents with facial erythema, flushing, telangiectasia, and papulopustules on the central face. It is triggered by vasodilatory stimuli. Option A is CORRECT. Trigger avoidance is central to rosacea management: alcohol, hot/spicy food, hot beverages, sunlight, extreme temperatures, and vasodilatory medications. CKS recommends topical metronidazole 0.75% gel as first-line pharmacological treatment for papulopustular rosacea. Topical azelaic acid 15% gel is an equally effective alternative. Sun protection (SPF 30+) is important. Oral doxycycline 40 mg OD is used for moderate-severe rosacea. Option B is INCORRECT — adapalene is a retinoid for acne; it worsens rosacea by causing irritation. Option C is INCORRECT — topical hydrocortisone worsens rosacea and should be avoided; steroids are contraindicated in rosacea. Option D is INCORRECT — trigger avoidance significantly reduces flare frequency; oral antibiotics are second-line for moderate disease. Option E is INCORRECT — rosacea is not fungal and not related to gluten sensitivity.

label: Lichen Planus
stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with intensely itchy, violaceous, flat-topped polygonal papules on her wrists and forearms. On close examination, a fine white lacy pattern (Wickham's striae) is visible on the surface. She also has buccal erosions. Which of the following statements about this condition is correct?
options: It is a T-cell-mediated autoimmune condition; oral lesions should be reviewed for malignant transformation
It is caused by Staphylococcus aureus and treated with flucloxacillin
It is a fungal infection requiring oral terbinafine
It is viral — treat with oral aciclovir
It always resolves within 2 weeks without treatment
correctAnswerText: It is a T-cell-mediated autoimmune condition; oral lesions should be reviewed for malignant transformation
explanation:
The 4 Ps of lichen planus: Purple, Pruritic, Polygonal, Papules — plus Wickham's striae and mucosal involvement confirm the diagnosis. Lichen planus is a T-cell-mediated autoimmune inflammatory condition.

Option A is CORRECT. Lichen planus involves dysregulated T-cell-mediated destruction of basal keratinocytes. Oral lichen planus (especially the erosive variant) has a small but real risk of malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma (~1–2% lifetime risk), requiring regular oral surveillance. Treatment: potent topical steroids, tacrolimus for mucosal disease.

Option B is INCORRECT — it is not bacterial.

Option C is INCORRECT — it is not fungal.

Option D is INCORRECT — it is not viral, though hepatitis C is an associated trigger in some populations.

Option E is INCORRECT — cutaneous lichen planus resolves over months to years but does NOT resolve in 2 weeks. Oral lichen planus can be chronic and refractory.

label: Rheumatoid Arthritis
stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with an 18-month history of symmetrical small joint pain, early morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour, and bilateral hand deformities. The image shows ulnar deviation of the fingers at the MCPJs, swan neck deformities, and subluxation. She is RF and anti-CCP positive. What is the most appropriate initial disease-modifying treatment?
options: Methotrexate (up to 25 mg/week) plus folic acid 5 mg once weekly
Prednisolone 40 mg daily as monotherapy
Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy
NSAIDs as sole disease-modifying treatment
Rituximab as first-line biologic
correctAnswerText: Methotrexate (up to 25 mg/week) plus folic acid 5 mg once weekly
explanation: The classic deformities of established rheumatoid arthritis (RA) — ulnar deviation, swan neck deformity, MCPJ subluxation — with symmetrical polyarthritis, prolonged morning stiffness, positive RF and anti-CCP confirm the diagnosis. Option A is CORRECT. NG100 recommends methotrexate as the anchor DMARD (disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drug) in RA. It should be started as early as possible. Folic acid 5 mg once weekly (not on the same day as methotrexate) reduces side effects (mucositis, hepatotoxicity). The T2T (treat-to-target) strategy aims for remission or low disease activity. Option B is INCORRECT — oral steroids are used as short-term bridging therapy during disease flares, not as primary DMARD. Long-term steroid monotherapy causes significant harm. Option C is INCORRECT — hydroxychloroquine has a mild effect and is used in combination for very mild disease; it is not first-line for established seropositive RA with deformity. Option D is INCORRECT — NSAIDs reduce inflammation and pain but have no disease-modifying effect. Option E is INCORRECT — biologics (rituximab, anti-TNF agents) are second-line after failure of ≥2 conventional DMARDs.

label: Gout
stem: A 58-year-old obese man presents with sudden onset severe pain, redness, and swelling of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra) that woke him from sleep. Serum urate is 520 μmol/L. The image shows the affected foot. He is on thiazide diuretics for hypertension. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily or colchicine 500 mcg 2–4 times daily
Start allopurinol immediately to lower urate
Oral prednisolone 30–35 mg daily for 5 days
Joint aspiration and intravenous antibiotics
IV colchicine
correctAnswerText: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily or colchicine 500 mcg 2–4 times daily
explanation: Acute podagra — sudden, severe MTP joint pain, erythema, and tenderness in a middle-aged obese man on diuretics — is the classic presentation of gout (urate crystal deposition). Option A is CORRECT. First-line treatment for acute gout is an NSAID (e.g. naproxen, indomethacin) or colchicine 500 mcg 2–4 times daily. CKS recommends choosing based on comorbidities — NSAIDs are contraindicated in renal impairment/PUD; colchicine is preferred then. A short course (7–10 days) relieves the attack. Option B is INCORRECT — allopurinol (urate-lowering therapy) must NOT be started during an acute attack as it can prolong or worsen the flare by mobilising urate crystals. Start 2–4 weeks after the acute episode settles. Option C is INCORRECT — prednisolone is an appropriate second-line option if NSAIDs and colchicine are both contraindicated; not first-line. Option D is INCORRECT — joint aspiration is appropriate to confirm diagnosis (urate crystals) if uncertain, but antibiotics are only for septic arthritis. Option E is INCORRECT — IV colchicine is not available in the UK and has a narrow therapeutic index.

label: Graves Disease
stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with weight loss, palpitations, heat intolerance, and anxiety over 3 months. The image shows bilateral proptosis and lid lag. TFTs: TSH <0.01 mU/L, free T4 42 pmol/L. TSH receptor antibodies are elevated. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Carbimazole 20–40 mg daily (titration regimen)
Propylthiouracil as first-line in all thyrotoxicosis
Radioactive iodine (I-131) immediately
Total thyroidectomy
Propranolol alone — antithyroid drugs are not needed
correctAnswerText: Carbimazole 20–40 mg daily (titration regimen)
explanation:
The clinical picture — proptosis (Graves' ophthalmopathy), thyrotoxicosis symptoms, suppressed TSH, elevated free T4, and positive TSH receptor antibodies — confirms Graves' disease, an autoimmune hyperthyroidism.

Option A is CORRECT. Carbimazole is first-line antithyroid drug treatment for Graves' disease in the UK. A titration regimen (starting 20–40 mg OD, reducing as TFTs normalise) or block-and-replace regimen is used. Warn about agranulocytosis (stop immediately if sore throat/fever, check FBC). Propranolol (or atenolol) is added initially to control symptoms.

Option B is INCORRECT — PTU is second-line (used in first trimester of pregnancy and thyroid storm) due to more severe hepatotoxicity risk compared to carbimazole.

Option C is INCORRECT — I-131 is contraindicated in active Graves' ophthalmopathy as it may worsen eye disease. It is considered for definitive treatment after medical management.

Option D is INCORRECT — thyroidectomy is reserved for large goitres causing compressive symptoms or failure of medical therapy.

Option E is INCORRECT — propranolol controls symptoms (tremor, palpitations) but does not treat the underlying thyrotoxicosis.

label: Neuropathic Diabetic Ulcer
stem: A 62-year-old man with a 20-year history of type 2 diabetes (poorly controlled, HbA1c 87 mmol/mol) presents with a painless ulcer under his right first metatarsal head. The image shows a punched-out ulcer with callus, surrounding erythema, and a probing test positive to bone. His foot is warm with present pulses. What is the most important next investigation?
options: X-ray foot and MRI foot to assess for osteomyelitis
Ankle-brachial pressure index
Doppler ultrasound for peripheral vascular disease
Random blood glucose only
Nerve conduction studies
correctAnswerText: X-ray foot and MRI foot to assess for osteomyelitis
explanation:
Neuropathic diabetic foot ulcers are punched out, painless, surrounded by callus, over pressure points, with warm foot and present pulses (contrast with ischaemic ulcers which are painful, pale, cold, with absent pulses). Probe-to-bone positive (reaching bone with a sterile probe) has ~90% specificity for osteomyelitis.

Option A is CORRECT. A probe-to-bone positive test in a diabetic foot ulcer has high predictive value for osteomyelitis. Foot X-ray (initial — shows bony destruction/periosteal reaction late) and MRI foot (gold standard — detects early marrow changes) are both required to assess for osteomyelitis before deciding on treatment (prolonged IV antibiotics ± surgical debridement/bone resection).

Option B is INCORRECT — ABPI measures ischaemia. This is a neuropathic ulcer with warm foot and present pulses, making vascular disease unlikely.

Option C is INCORRECT — Doppler is relevant for ischaemic ulcers, not the immediate priority here.

Option D is INCORRECT — blood glucose monitoring is important for general management but does not assess osteomyelitis.

Option E is INCORRECT — nerve conduction studies confirm peripheral neuropathy but do not guide acute management of the current ulcer.

label: Thyroid Goitre
stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a painless, diffuse neck swelling that moves on swallowing and protrusion of the tongue. The image shows a visible anterior neck mass. She has no symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism. TFTs are normal. What is the most appropriate investigation?
options: Thyroid ultrasound to characterise the goitre and identify nodules
CT neck with contrast immediately
Fine needle aspiration cytology without imaging
Nuclear thyroid scan as first-line investigation
Reassure and discharge — normal TFTs confirm benign goitre
correctAnswerText: Thyroid ultrasound to characterise the goitre and identify nodules
explanation:
A euthyroid (normal TFTs) diffuse goitre requires characterisation to identify any sinister features — nodules suspicious for thyroid cancer, cystic components, or compressive elements.

Option A is CORRECT. Thyroid ultrasound is the first-line investigation for any goitre or thyroid nodule. It characterises nodules (solid vs cystic, echogenicity, vascularity) and guides FNA cytology using the U1–U5 scoring system (U1 = normal, U5 = highly suspicious for malignancy). Ultrasound is safe, quick, and inexpensive.

Option B is INCORRECT — CT neck with contrast is second-line (for retrosternal extension, airway compression assessment) after USS. Iodinated contrast can precipitate thyrotoxicosis.

Option C is INCORRECT — FNA is guided by USS findings (U3–U5 lesions warrant FNA). Blind FNA without imaging is inappropriate.

Option D is INCORRECT — radionuclide scan is used for hyperthyroid states to distinguish hot (autonomous) from cold (potentially malignant) nodules. Not first-line in euthyroid patients.

Option E is INCORRECT — normal TFTs do not exclude malignancy. Thyroid cancer is often euthyroid.

label: Kawasaki Disease
stem: A 3-year-old boy has had a fever for 7 days that has not responded to antibiotics. The image shows bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, and erythema of the lips. He also has a diffuse maculopapular rash, cervical lymphadenopathy (>1.5 cm), and erythema/oedema of the hands and feet. What is the most important complication to screen for?
options: Coronary artery aneurysms — perform echocardiography
Renal vein thrombosis — urgent renal Doppler
Meningitis — perform lumbar puncture
Myocarditis only — arrange ECG
Hepatitis — LFTs are the priority investigation
correctAnswerText: Coronary artery aneurysms — perform echocardiography
explanation:
This child meets the criteria for Kawasaki disease (KD): fever ≥5 days + ≥4 of: bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, oral changes (strawberry tongue, cracked lips), polymorphous rash, extremity changes (erythema/oedema), and cervical lymphadenopathy >1.5 cm.

Option A is CORRECT. The most feared complication of Kawasaki disease is coronary artery aneurysms (CAA), occurring in up to 25% of untreated cases. Treatment with aspirin + IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg single infusion reduces this risk to <5%. Echocardiography is essential at diagnosis and repeated at 2 and 6–8 weeks. Giant aneurysms (>8 mm) carry risk of thrombosis and MI.

Option B is INCORRECT — renal vein thrombosis is not a complication of Kawasaki disease.

Option C is INCORRECT — while aseptic meningitis can occur in KD, LP is not the priority. KD is not primarily a CNS disease.

Option D is INCORRECT — ECG is useful to detect conduction abnormalities but coronary artery assessment requires echocardiography.

Option E is INCORRECT — liver involvement can occur (cholestatic jaundice, elevated LFTs) but is not the priority concern.

label: Otitis Externa
stem: A 25-year-old swimmer presents with a 5-day history of right ear pain, itch, and discharge. Examination reveals tenderness on tragal pressure and pulling the pinna, an oedematous, erythematous external auditory canal with debris. The tympanic membrane is visible and intact. What is the first-line treatment?
options: Topical acetic acid 2% spray or topical antibiotic/steroid ear drops (e.g. dexamethasone/neomycin)
Oral amoxicillin for 7 days
Oral ciprofloxacin immediately
Ear syringing to remove debris
Systemic antifungal (fluconazole) — fungal otitis externa
correctAnswerText: Topical acetic acid 2% spray or topical antibiotic/steroid ear drops (e.g. dexamethasone/neomycin)
explanation: Otitis externa — diffuse inflammation of the external auditory canal — presents with pain, itch, discharge, and tenderness to traction/tragal pressure. Causative organisms are usually Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus. "Swimmer's ear" is a classic risk factor. Option A is CORRECT. CKS recommends topical treatment as first-line: acetic acid 2% spray (antibacterial and antifungal) is equally effective and avoids antibiotic resistance. Topical antibiotic-steroid combinations (ciprofloxacin/dexamethasone or neomycin/dexamethasone) are alternatives. Keep ear dry. Option B is INCORRECT — oral antibiotics are not required for uncomplicated otitis externa; topical agents achieve much higher local concentrations. Option C is INCORRECT — oral ciprofloxacin is reserved for malignant (necrotising) otitis externa in immunocompromised patients (diabetics, elderly) or when cellulitis spreads beyond the canal. Option D is INCORRECT — ear syringing in acute otitis externa causes significant pain and may worsen inflammation. Option E is INCORRECT — fungal otitis externa (Aspergillus/Candida) presents with a whitish membrane/debris and is treated with topical clotrimazole. The clinical picture here is bacterial.

label: Peritonsillar Abscess
stem: A 22-year-old man presents with 4 days of severe worsening sore throat, inability to open his mouth fully (trismus), muffled "hot potato" voice, and dysphagia. The image shows unilateral tonsillar swelling with deviation of the uvula to the right, and a bulging of the left soft palate. What is the correct management?
options: Needle aspiration of the abscess plus IV amoxicillin/metronidazole
Oral amoxicillin and discharge
Immediate tonsillectomy under general anaesthetic
Systemic antifungal — this is oral candidiasis
Reassurance — this is severe tonsillitis that will resolve with rest
correctAnswerText: Needle aspiration of the abscess plus IV amoxicillin/metronidazole
explanation:
The unilateral tonsillar swelling with uvular deviation, trismus, and muffled voice with dysphagia is classic peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) — a collection of pus between the tonsillar capsule and superior constrictor muscle.

Option A is CORRECT. Treatment requires both drainage and antibiotics. Needle aspiration (or incision and drainage) is performed under local anaesthetic — this immediately relieves symptoms and confirms pus. IV/oral co-amoxiclav or amoxicillin + metronidazole covers Streptococcus and anaerobes. Hospital admission is appropriate for IV fluids and analgesia if unable to swallow.

Option B is INCORRECT — oral antibiotics alone do not drain the abscess; aspiration/I&D is essential for cure.

Option C is INCORRECT — interval (elective) tonsillectomy is considered after ≥2 quinsy episodes, not as emergency treatment of an acute abscess with trismus.

Option D is INCORRECT — oral candidiasis presents with white plaques, not tonsillar swelling with uvular deviation.

Option E is INCORRECT — peritonsillar abscess will not resolve without drainage; airway compromise can occur if untreated.

label: Exudative Tonsillitis
stem: A 19-year-old woman presents with a 4-day history of severe sore throat, difficulty swallowing, fever (38.5°C), and extreme fatigue. Examination reveals bilateral tonsillar enlargement with white exudate, cervical lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly. The image shows the oropharynx. Monospot test is positive. What is the most important drug to avoid?
options: Amoxicillin — causes a widespread maculopapular rash in EBV infection
Paracetamol — worsens fever in EBV
Ibuprofen — contraindicated in all throat infections
Aciclovir — contraindicated in glandular fever
Prednisolone — never used in EBV infection
correctAnswerText: Amoxicillin — causes a widespread maculopapular rash in EBV infection
explanation:
The clinical picture — bilateral exudative tonsillitis, massive cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, extreme fatigue, and positive monospot — is diagnostic of infectious mononucleosis (glandular fever) caused by Epstein-Barr virus (EBV).

Option A is CORRECT. Amoxicillin (and ampicillin) MUST be avoided in suspected EBV/glandular fever because it causes a widespread maculopapular rash in ~90–100% of cases (the amoxicillin-EBV rash). This is the most important prescribing pitfall. Management is supportive: adequate hydration, analgesia (paracetamol/ibuprofen), rest, and advice about splenomegaly (avoid contact sports for 4–6 weeks due to splenic rupture risk).

Option B is INCORRECT — paracetamol is appropriate and safe for symptom relief.

Option C is INCORRECT — ibuprofen is appropriate unless there are contraindications (renal disease, asthma). It is not specifically contraindicated in EBV.

Option D is INCORRECT — aciclovir is not effective against EBV and is not contraindicated per se; it just has no benefit.

Option E is INCORRECT — prednisolone is occasionally used for severe tonsillar swelling threatening the airway.

label: Obstructive Jaundice
stem: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive painless jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and a 6 kg weight loss over 8 weeks. On examination, there is deep jaundice and a palpable, non-tender gallbladder. Bilirubin is 280 μmol/L (conjugated). What is Courvoisier's law and what does it suggest here?
options: A palpable, non-tender gallbladder in the presence of jaundice is unlikely to be due to gallstones — suggests carcinoma of the head of pancreas
Jaundice plus palpable gallbladder always means choledocholithiasis
Courvoisier's law states that jaundice with a tender gallbladder indicates pancreatitis
A non-tender gallbladder with jaundice is pathognomonic of hepatitis
Courvoisier's law is no longer clinically useful
correctAnswerText: A palpable, non-tender gallbladder in the presence of jaundice is unlikely to be due to gallstones — suggests carcinoma of the head of pancreas
explanation:
This presentation — painless progressive jaundice, pale stools, dark urine, weight loss, and a palpable non-tender gallbladder — is the classic presentation of carcinoma of the head of the pancreas causing biliary obstruction.

Option A is CORRECT. Courvoisier's law states: "A palpable, non-tender gallbladder in a jaundiced patient is unlikely to be due to gallstones." Rationale: chronic gallstone disease causes gallbladder fibrosis/thickening (cannot distend). A dilated gallbladder implies a new/progressive obstruction — most commonly pancreatic head carcinoma. Investigation: CT abdomen/pelvis (staging), MRCP, CA 19-9. The 5-year survival for pancreatic cancer is <10%, making early referral critical.

Option B is INCORRECT — gallstones typically cause a contracted, fibrosed gallbladder; Courvoisier's law explicitly contradicts this.

Option C is INCORRECT — pancreatitis causes epigastric pain radiating to the back, not painless jaundice.

Option D is INCORRECT — hepatitis causes hepatocellular (unconjugated) jaundice with tender hepatomegaly, not a palpable gallbladder.

Option E is INCORRECT — Courvoisier's sign remains a useful clinical pointer to obstructive jaundice from malignancy.

label: Cirrhosis Stigmata
stem: A 54-year-old man with a long history of alcohol excess presents with jaundice, ascites, and confusion. The image shows spider naevi, palmar erythema, and gynaecomastia. His prothrombin time is prolonged. His Child-Pugh class is C. Which of the following best explains the gynaecomastia in cirrhosis?
options: Impaired hepatic metabolism of oestrogens combined with reduced testosterone production
Adrenal insufficiency causing excess ACTH stimulation
Chronic alcohol suppressing LH and FSH equally
Direct hepatic synthesis of oestradiol
Gynaecomastia is unrelated to liver disease
correctAnswerText: Impaired hepatic metabolism of oestrogens combined with reduced testosterone production
explanation:
The stigmata of chronic liver disease include: spider naevi, palmar erythema, leukonychia, clubbing, jaundice, caput medusae, ascites, gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy, and hepatic encephalopathy.

Option A is CORRECT. In cirrhosis, the failing liver cannot adequately metabolise oestrogens (normally converted to less active forms). Simultaneously, low albumin and Leydig cell dysfunction reduce testosterone. The resulting increased oestrogen-to-androgen ratio stimulates breast tissue, causing gynaecomastia. Spironolactone (used to treat ascites) can worsen gynaecomastia as a side effect.

Option B is INCORRECT — ACTH excess (Cushing's) causes virilisation and truncal obesity, not gynaecomastia.

Option C is INCORRECT — while alcohol has gonadotoxic effects on testosterone, this alone does not explain gynaecomastia without the oestrogen metabolism component.

Option D is INCORRECT — the liver does not synthesise oestradiol directly; it metabolises and inactivates circulating oestrogens.

Option E is INCORRECT — gynaecomastia is a well-recognised sign of chronic liver disease/cirrhosis.

label: Small Bowel Obstruction
stem: A 65-year-old woman with a history of previous appendicectomy presents with colicky central abdominal pain, vomiting, abdominal distension, and absolute constipation for 24 hours. The AXR is shown with multiple dilated loops of central bowel with valvulae conniventes visible, the largest measuring 4.5 cm. What is the most likely cause?
options: Adhesion-related small bowel obstruction
Large bowel obstruction from sigmoid carcinoma
Paralytic ileus
Caecal volvulus
Mesenteric ischaemia
correctAnswerText: Adhesion-related small bowel obstruction
explanation:
The AXR shows dilated central small bowel loops (>3 cm) with valvulae conniventes (folds crossing the full diameter, in contrast to haustra which are partial) — a classic pattern of small bowel obstruction (SBO). The previous abdominal surgery is a key clue.

Option A is CORRECT. Post-operative adhesions account for ~75% of SBO in the UK. Previous appendicectomy leaves adhesion bands that can cause bowel kinking/strangulation years later. Management: IV fluids, NG tube decompression, and surgical review. Urgent CT abdomen is essential to assess for ischaemia or closed loop obstruction requiring emergency surgery.

Option B is INCORRECT — large bowel obstruction causes peripheral dilated large bowel (haustra, not valvulae conniventes) with a "frame" pattern on AXR.

Option C is INCORRECT — paralytic ileus causes generalized bowel dilatation (both small and large bowel) without the mechanical colicky pattern; usually post-operative.

Option D is INCORRECT — caecal volvulus causes a single dilated loop arising from the right iliac fossa.

Option E is INCORRECT — mesenteric ischaemia typically presents with severe pain out of proportion to signs, not classic SBO features.

label: Sigmoid Volvulus
stem: An 80-year-old man from a care home presents with acute colicky left-sided abdominal pain, massive abdominal distension, and absolute constipation for 36 hours. He has a history of chronic constipation. The AXR is shown with a massively dilated loop of bowel arising from the left iliac fossa with the appearance of an "inverted U" or "coffee bean" sign. What is the first-line treatment?
options: Rigid sigmoidoscopy or flexible sigmoidoscopy for deflation
Emergency Hartmann's procedure
IV neostigmine to stimulate peristalsis
Fleet enema per rectum
CT-guided percutaneous drainage
correctAnswerText: Rigid sigmoidoscopy or flexible sigmoidoscopy for deflation
explanation:
The "coffee bean" sign (inverted U-shaped dilated loop of large bowel, apex pointing to the right upper quadrant, arising from the left iliac fossa) is pathognomonic of sigmoid volvulus — twisting of the sigmoid colon on its mesentery.

Option A is CORRECT. For uncomplicated sigmoid volvulus (no evidence of perforation/peritonitis/ischaemia), the first-line treatment is endoscopic deflation via rigid or flexible sigmoidoscopy. This untwists the volvulus and decompresses the bowel. A rectal flatus tube can be left in situ. Recurrence rate is high (~90% without surgery), so elective sigmoid colectomy is planned after recovery.

Option B is INCORRECT — emergency surgery (Hartmann's) is reserved for failed endoscopic deflation, gangrenous bowel, or perforation.

Option C is INCORRECT — neostigmine is used for Ogilvie's syndrome (pseudo-obstruction), not mechanical volvulus.

Option D is INCORRECT — enemas cannot untwist a volvulus and may cause perforation.

Option E is INCORRECT — percutaneous drainage is not appropriate for a mechanical obstruction.

label: Dupuytren Contracture
stem: A 62-year-old man of Northern European ancestry presents with progressive inability to fully extend his right ring and little fingers. The image shows thickened palmar skin, palpable cords, and fixed flexion contracture of the ring and little fingers at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. He is a long-standing alcohol misuser. Which statement about this condition is correct?
options: It is caused by fibromatosis of the palmar fascia; the ring finger is most commonly affected and indications for treatment include MCP joint contracture ≥30°
It is caused by extensor tendon rupture and requires immediate surgical repair
It is treated with systemic corticosteroids as first-line
It is a sign of flexor digitorum profundus contracture and physiotherapy is curative
It is associated with carpal tunnel syndrome only and not with alcohol excess
correctAnswerText: It is caused by fibromatosis of the palmar fascia; the ring finger is most commonly affected and indications for treatment include MCP joint contracture ≥30°
explanation:
Dupuytren's contracture (palmar fibromatosis) is progressive fibrotic thickening of the palmar fascia causing fixed flexion deformity, most commonly of the ring and little fingers.

Option A is CORRECT. Dupuytren's is a fibromatosis of the palmar fascia, not tendon rupture. Risk factors include: Northern European ancestry, male sex, older age, diabetes, alcohol excess, epilepsy (phenytoin), HIV, and hand trauma/vibrating tools. The ring finger is most commonly affected. Treatment is indicated when MCP joint contracture reaches ≥30° or PIP contracture is present: options include needle fasciotomy, collagenase (Clostridium histolyticum) injection (Xiapex), or surgical fasciectomy.

Option B is INCORRECT — it is a fascial, not tendon, problem. The flexor tendons are intact.

Option C is INCORRECT — steroids have no evidence for Dupuytren's contracture.

Option D is INCORRECT — FDP contracture is a different entity. Physiotherapy cannot reverse fibrosis once established.

Option E is INCORRECT — Dupuytren's is associated with both alcohol excess AND multiple other conditions; association with carpal tunnel is not its defining feature.

label: Copd Emphysema
stem: A 68-year-old man with a 45 pack-year smoking history presents with a 6-month history of progressive dyspnoea on exertion and a chronic productive cough. His FEV1/FVC ratio is 0.58. The chest X-ray above is obtained. Which of the following radiological findings is most consistent with the diagnosis of COPD/emphysema?
options: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy
Hyperinflated lungs with flattened hemidiaphragms
Unilateral pleural effusion
Bilateral interstitial shadowing in the lower zones
Cardiomegaly with upper lobe blood diversion
correctAnswerText: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened hemidiaphragms
explanation:
The CXR demonstrates the hallmark features of COPD/emphysema: hyperinflated lung fields with increased lucency (air trapping) and flattened hemidiaphragms sitting below the anterior end of the 6th rib.

Option A (Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy) is INCORRECT — this pattern suggests sarcoidosis or lymphoma, not COPD.

Option B (Hyperinflated lungs with flattened hemidiaphragms) is CORRECT. Air trapping from loss of elastic recoil pushes the diaphragms flat and increases the AP diameter, giving the classic barrel chest appearance. Diaphragm flattening below the 6th anterior rib is the key diagnostic sign.

Option C (Unilateral pleural effusion) is INCORRECT — not a typical feature of COPD; should prompt investigation for another cause.

Option D (Bilateral basal interstitial shadowing) is INCORRECT — this pattern is seen in pulmonary fibrosis or heart failure, not COPD.

Option E (Cardiomegaly with upper lobe diversion) is INCORRECT — this is the CXR pattern of left ventricular failure.

Clinical pearl: Do not confuse the increased lucency of emphysema with a pneumothorax — in emphysema, lung markings extend symmetrically to the periphery on both sides.

label: Oral Candidiasis
stem: A 58-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 85 cells/µL, not on antiretrovirals) presents with a 2-week history of a sore mouth and difficulty swallowing. Examination reveals white curd-like plaques on the tongue and buccal mucosa that can be scraped off, leaving a red bleeding base. The clinical photograph above is shown. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Nystatin oral suspension 100,000 units/mL, 1 mL four times daily for 7 days
Fluconazole 150 mg oral single dose
Fluconazole 50 mg orally once daily for 7–14 days
Clotrimazole 1% oral gel four times daily for 14 days
IV amphotericin B
correctAnswerText: Fluconazole 50 mg orally once daily for 7–14 days
explanation: This clinical photograph shows oral candidiasis (thrush): white curd-like plaques on the tongue that are characteristic of Candida albicans infection. The plaques scrape off to reveal an erythematous, bleeding base — distinguishing thrush from leukoplakia (which cannot be scraped off). Option A (Nystatin suspension) is INCORRECT as first-line in immunocompromised patients — nystatin is appropriate for mild disease in immunocompetent patients but is less effective in those with significant immunosuppression (CD4 <200). Option B (Fluconazole 150 mg single dose) is INCORRECT — a single dose is used for vaginal candidiasis; oropharyngeal candidiasis in immunocompromised patients requires a 7–14 day course. Option C (Fluconazole 50 mg once daily for 7–14 days) is CORRECT. This is the /BNF recommended first-line systemic treatment for oropharyngeal candidiasis in immunocompromised patients. A longer course is used for HIV patients with low CD4 counts. Option D (Clotrimazole gel) may be used in mild disease in immunocompetent patients but is insufficient here given the degree of immunosuppression and dysphagia suggesting oesophageal involvement. Option E (IV amphotericin B) is reserved for systemic/invasive candidiasis or fluconazole-resistant disease. Clinical pearl: Oropharyngeal candidiasis in an adult should prompt investigation for underlying immunosuppression — HIV, haematological malignancy, prolonged corticosteroid use, or diabetes.

label: Rosacea
stem: A 52-year-old fair-skinned man presents with a 3-year history of facial flushing, persistent central facial redness, and visible blood vessels across his nose and cheeks. He reports that symptoms worsen with alcohol, hot drinks, and sun exposure. The clinical photograph above is shown. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his persistent facial erythema?
options: Topical metronidazole 0.75% gel twice daily
Topical brimonidine 0.33% gel once daily
Oral doxycycline 40 mg modified-release once daily
Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream twice daily
Oral isotretinoin 0.5 mg/kg/day
correctAnswerText: Topical brimonidine 0.33% gel once daily
explanation: This image shows rosacea subtype I (erythematotelangiectatic rosacea): persistent central facial erythema with telangiectasia over the nose and cheeks, without papules or pustules. Option A (Topical metronidazole) is INCORRECT as first-line here — metronidazole targets inflammatory rosacea (subtype II with papules/pustules), not erythema alone. Option B (Topical brimonidine 0.33% gel) is CORRECT. Brimonidine is an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that causes vasoconstriction. It is the /BAD-recommended first-line treatment specifically for persistent facial erythema in rosacea, acting within 30 minutes and lasting up to 12 hours. Option C (Oral doxycycline 40 mg MR) is used for moderate-to-severe inflammatory rosacea with papulopustular involvement, not for erythema alone. Option D (Topical hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED in rosacea — topical corticosteroids cause short-term improvement but rebound worsening and can precipitate steroid rosacea on withdrawal. Option E (Oral isotretinoin) is reserved for severe, refractory rosacea unresponsive to multiple other treatments. Clinical pearl: Common rosacea triggers include alcohol, hot drinks, spicy food, sunlight, exercise, and temperature extremes — lifestyle modification and trigger avoidance are essential alongside pharmacological treatment.

label: Onychomycosis
stem: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with a 9-month history of a thickened, yellowed, crumbling toenail. The clinical photograph is shown. Nail clippings confirm Trichophyton rubrum on mycology. He takes metformin and simvastatin. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Topical amorolfine 5% nail lacquer once weekly for 6 months
Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 6 weeks
Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 12–16 weeks
Oral itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 7 days per month for 3 months (pulse)
No treatment; reassure and advise foot hygiene
correctAnswerText: Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 12–16 weeks
explanation: This image shows onychomycosis (tinea unguium): subungual hyperkeratosis with yellow-white discolouration and partial onycholysis, confirmed by positive mycology for Trichophyton rubrum — the most common causative organism. Option A (Topical amorolfine alone) is INCORRECT for established toenail infection with matrix involvement — topical agents cannot penetrate the nail plate sufficiently and are only suitable for mild distal disease without matrix involvement. Option B (Terbinafine 6 weeks) is the correct duration for fingernail onychomycosis, not toenails. Option C (Terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12–16 weeks) is CORRECT. This is the /BNF first-line treatment for dermatophyte toenail onychomycosis. Check LFTs before starting — terbinafine is hepatotoxic. A key PLAB distinction: fingernails need 6 weeks, toenails need 12–16 weeks. Option D (Itraconazole pulse) is an alternative for patients intolerant of terbinafine, or for non-dermatophyte/Candida infections. Option E (No treatment) may be appropriate for asymptomatic elderly patients without comorbidities, but is incorrect here — diabetes increases the risk of secondary bacterial infection, cellulitis, and spread. Clinical pearl: Always confirm with mycology before prescribing systemic antifungals. Psoriatic nail disease can closely mimic onychomycosis.

label: Atrial Flutter
stem: A 61-year-old man with hypertension and mild mitral regurgitation presents with a 4-hour history of palpitations and mild breathlessness. BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 150 bpm regular, SpO₂ 97%. The ECG above is shown. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate initial management?
options: Atrial fibrillation — metoprolol 5 mg IV
Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — bisoprolol 5 mg orally for rate control
Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — immediate DC cardioversion
SVT — adenosine 6 mg IV bolus
Ventricular tachycardia — amiodarone 300 mg IV
correctAnswerText: Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — bisoprolol 5 mg orally for rate control
explanation: This ECG shows classic atrial flutter: regular sawtooth flutter waves (F waves) at ~300 bpm, best seen in leads II, III, aVF and V1, with a ventricular rate of ~150 bpm due to 2:1 AV conduction. A regular tachycardia at exactly 150 bpm should always raise suspicion for atrial flutter with 2:1 block. Option A (AF with metoprolol IV) is INCORRECT — AF produces an irregularly irregular rhythm with no organised atrial activity, not the regular sawtooth pattern seen here. Option B (Atrial flutter 2:1 — bisoprolol orally) is CORRECT for this haemodynamically stable patient. Rate control with a beta-blocker (or rate-limiting calcium channel blocker such as diltiazem or verapamil) is first-line NG196 for stable atrial flutter. Option C (DC cardioversion) is indicated if haemodynamically unstable (hypotension, pulmonary oedema, syncope) or for elective rhythm control after anticoagulation assessment — not first-line in a stable patient. Option D (Adenosine for SVT) is INCORRECT as definitive treatment. Adenosine increases AV block and can transiently unmask flutter waves (useful diagnostically) but does not terminate flutter. Option E (Amiodarone for VT) is INCORRECT — VT produces a broad complex tachycardia; this ECG shows narrow complexes. Clinical pearl: HR of exactly 150 bpm = atrial flutter with 2:1 block until proven otherwise. Consider anticoagulation assessment in the same way as AF.

label: Lvh
stem: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of poorly controlled hypertension attends for routine review. BP today is 168/102 mmHg. He is asymptomatic. The ECG above is obtained. Which of the following best describes the finding and its clinical significance?
options: Right ventricular hypertrophy — suggests cor pulmonale; arrange CTPA
Left ventricular hypertrophy — confirms hypertensive end-organ damage; intensify antihypertensive therapy
Left bundle branch block — urgent cardiology referral for pacing assessment
Anterior STEMI — call the cath lab immediately
Normal ECG variant — no action required
correctAnswerText: Left ventricular hypertrophy — confirms hypertensive end-organ damage; intensify antihypertensive therapy
explanation:
This ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH): tall R waves in lateral leads (I, aVL, V4–V6), deep S waves in right precordial leads (V1–V2), and a lateral strain pattern (ST depression with T-wave inversion in V5–V6, I, aVL). Diagnostic voltage criteria:
• Sokolow–Lyon: S(V1) + R(V5 or V6) ≥35 mm
• Cornell: R(aVL) + S(V3) ≥28 mm in men Option A (RVH) is INCORRECT — RVH shows tall R in V1, right axis deviation, and deep S in lateral leads — the opposite pattern. Option B (LVH — intensify treatment) is CORRECT. LVH is a -defined marker of hypertensive end-organ damage, significantly increasing cardiovascular risk. It mandates intensification of antihypertensives targeting BP <130/80 mmHg and consideration of echocardiography. Option C (LBBB) is INCORRECT — LBBB requires broad QRS >120 ms with M-pattern in V5/V6; QRS here is narrow. Option D (Anterior STEMI) is INCORRECT — there is no ST elevation; the lateral ST depression is the LV strain pattern secondary to hypertrophy. Option E (Normal variant) is INCORRECT — voltage criteria are clearly exceeded. Clinical pearl: LVH strain pattern (lateral ST depression/T-inversion) is frequently confused with lateral ischaemia — context is key: longstanding hypertension + voltage criteria = LVH strain.

label: Geographic Tongue
stem: A 28-year-old woman attends her GP concerned about the appearance of her tongue, which she noticed 2 weeks ago. She reports mild sensitivity to spicy and acidic foods but no pain at rest, no fever, and no difficulty swallowing. She is otherwise well with no significant past medical history. Examination reveals the finding shown. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Prescribe oral fluconazole for 7 days
Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral surgery
Reassure and advise avoidance of known triggers
Take a tongue biopsy to exclude dysplasia
Prescribe topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellets)
correctAnswerText: Reassure and advise avoidance of known triggers
explanation:
This image shows geographic tongue (benign migratory glossitis): irregularly shaped erythematous patches with white serpiginous borders on the dorsal tongue, representing areas of desquamated filiform papillae. The map-like pattern migrates over days to weeks.

Option C is CORRECT. Geographic tongue is a benign, self-limiting inflammatory condition. No treatment is required. Management is reassurance plus avoidance of triggers (spicy/acidic food, alcohol, tobacco).

Option A (Fluconazole) is INCORRECT — oral candidiasis presents as white pseudomembranous plaques that wipe off, leaving a raw red base. Geographic tongue has erythematous patches with fixed white borders and no removable coating.

Option B (2WW referral) is INCORRECT — geographic tongue is benign. Red flags for oral cancer include indurated non-healing ulcers and persistent white/red patches (leukoplakia/erythroplakia) for >3 weeks. None are present here.

Option D (Biopsy) is INCORRECT — biopsy is not indicated for classic geographic tongue without suspicious features.

Option E (Topical corticosteroids) may reduce symptoms in severe symptomatic cases but is not first-line for a mildly symptomatic presentation.

Clinical pearl: Geographic tongue is associated with psoriasis and atopy. The key distinction from candidiasis — candidal plaques are white and removable; geographic tongue patches are smooth red areas that cannot be wiped off.

label: Cyanosis
stem: A 68-year-old man with a long history of COPD presents to the emergency department with worsening breathlessness over 3 days. He smokes 20 cigarettes/day. Examination of the hands reveals the finding shown. Oxygen saturation is 84% on air. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min
Controlled oxygen therapy titrated to SpO₂ 88–92%, with arterial blood gas monitoring
Salbutamol nebuliser only and reassess in 1 hour
Oral prednisolone 40 mg and discharge with community follow-up
Urgent CT pulmonary angiogram to exclude pulmonary embolism
correctAnswerText: Controlled oxygen therapy titrated to SpO₂ 88–92%, with arterial blood gas monitoring
explanation:
The image shows peripheral cyanosis: blue-purple discolouration of the nail beds and fingertips due to increased deoxygenated haemoglobin in peripheral capillaries.

Option B is CORRECT. In known COPD (a CO₂-retaining condition), controlled oxygen therapy targeting SpO₂ 88–92% is mandated. A subset of COPD patients have chronic hypercapnia and depend on their hypoxic drive for ventilation. Uncontrolled high-flow oxygen suppresses this drive, worsens hypercapnia, and precipitates type 2 respiratory failure. A Venturi mask at 24–28% FiO₂ is the delivery device of choice, with ABG to monitor CO₂.

Option A (15 L/min high-flow O₂) is INCORRECT and potentially lethal in COPD — this is the standard approach for non-COPD patients (e.g. pneumonia, trauma) where target SpO₂ is 94–98%.

Option C (Salbutamol only) is insufficient for SpO₂ 84%.

Option D (Oral prednisolone + discharge) is dangerous — this patient requires hospital admission.

Option E (CTPA) is not the immediate priority; correct the acute hypoxaemia first.

Clinical pearl: The single highest-yield oxygen target distinction in PLAB — COPD = 88–92%; everyone else = 94–98%. Always check the diagnosis before reaching for the oxygen mask.

label: Herpes Labialis
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 2-day history of painful, tingling blisters on her upper lip. She reports a similar episode 6 months ago that resolved spontaneously. She is otherwise well with no fever. The appearance is shown. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral aciclovir 200 mg five times daily for 5 days
Topical aciclovir 5% cream applied five times daily for 5 days
Oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose
Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream twice daily
Reassure — no treatment required; will resolve in 48 hours
correctAnswerText: Topical aciclovir 5% cream applied five times daily for 5 days
explanation: The image shows herpes labialis (cold sore): a cluster of painful vesicles on the vermilion border of the lip, caused by reactivation of latent HSV-1. Option B is CORRECT. CKS recommends topical aciclovir 5% cream five times daily for 5 days as first-line treatment in an immunocompetent patient. Efficacy is greatest when started at the first sign of tingling (prodrome). Option A (Oral aciclovir) is reserved for severe or widespread HSV, immunocompromised patients, eczema herpeticum, or herpetic keratoconjunctivitis — not a straightforward cold sore. Option C (Fluconazole) is an antifungal — ineffective against HSV. Option D (Topical hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED in viral infections — steroids suppress local immunity and may worsen HSV spread. Option E (Reassure only) is INCORRECT — untreated cold sores take 7–10 days to resolve; topical aciclovir shortens duration and reduces symptoms. Clinical pearl: The prodromal tingling before blisters appear is the optimal treatment window. Counsel patients to keep topical aciclovir at home and start it at first tingle.

label: Aphthous Ulcer
stem: A 19-year-old woman presents to her GP with a painful lesion on the inside of her lower lip for 4 days, shown above. She reports this is her third episode in 6 months. She has no systemic symptoms, no weight loss, and no diarrhoea. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Topical chlorhexidine mouthwash and topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellet)
Oral aciclovir 200 mg five times daily for 5 days
Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral surgery
Full blood count, B12, folate, ferritin, and anti-TTG antibodies
Topical aciclovir cream applied to the ulcer
correctAnswerText: Topical chlorhexidine mouthwash and topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellet)
explanation:
The image shows a minor aphthous ulcer: a well-defined, round/oval shallow ulcer with a white/grey pseudomembranous base and an erythematous halo on the labial mucosa. Option A is CORRECT. First-line management CKS:
• Topical chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash — reduces secondary infection and pain
• Topical corticosteroid — hydrocortisone oromucosal 2.5 mg pellet four times daily (or beclometasone inhaler as mouthwash) — shortens healing time Option B (Oral aciclovir) is INCORRECT — aphthous ulcers are NOT caused by HSV. Herpetic ulcers are smaller, more numerous, preceded by vesicles, and occur on keratinised mucosa (hard palate, gingiva); aphthous ulcers occur on non-keratinised mucosa (labial, buccal, floor of mouth). Option C (2WW referral) is INCORRECT — red flags for oral cancer requiring urgent referral are: unexplained ulcer persisting >3 weeks, induration, associated lymphadenopathy, or risk factors (smoking, alcohol). None apply here. Option D (Bloods) is appropriate for recurrent aphthous ulcers to exclude underlying causes (iron, B12/folate deficiency, coeliac disease), but treatment should be initiated concurrently. Option E (Topical aciclovir) is INCORRECT — aciclovir is antiviral; aphthous ulcers have no viral aetiology. Clinical pearl: Location is the key distinction — aphthous on non-keratinised (mobile) mucosa; herpetic on keratinised (fixed) mucosa such as the hard palate and attached gingiva.

label: Tinea Pedis
stem: A 24-year-old male university student presents with a 3-week history of itchy, peeling, and macerated skin between his toes and on the sole of his foot, as shown. He uses a communal shower at his gym. There is no nail involvement. What is the first-line treatment?
options: Topical clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks
Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 6 weeks
Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream twice daily for 2 weeks
Oral fluconazole 150 mg once weekly for 4 weeks
Topical fusidic acid cream twice daily for 7 days
correctAnswerText: Topical clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks
explanation: The image shows tinea pedis (athlete's foot): scaling, maceration, and peeling of the interdigital web spaces and plantar surface, caused by dermatophyte fungi (most commonly Trichophyton rubrum). Option A is CORRECT. Topical antifungal is first-line for tinea pedis without nail involvement. CKS recommends clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks (or terbinafine 1% cream once daily for 1 week — shorter, equally effective). Option B (Oral terbinafine) is second-line — reserved for topical treatment failure, extensive/moccasin-type tinea pedis, or onychomycosis (nail involvement). Not appropriate as initial therapy here. Option C (Topical hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED as monotherapy in fungal infections. Steroids suppress local immunity, worsen tinea, and can cause tinea incognito — an atypical altered appearance that delays diagnosis. Option D (Oral fluconazole) is second-line and not indicated as initial therapy for uncomplicated tinea pedis. Option E (Topical fusidic acid) is an antibacterial — ineffective against dermatophyte fungi. Clinical pearl: Always consider fungal infection before prescribing topical steroids for an itchy rash on the foot. Counsel on hygiene: dry thoroughly between toes, wear cotton socks, change footwear regularly, avoid communal barefoot areas.

label: Oral Leukoplakia
stem: A 58-year-old man who smokes 20 cigarettes per day and drinks 30 units of alcohol per week is referred by his dentist. He has a painless white patch on the lateral border of his tongue that cannot be wiped off, present for 6 weeks, as shown. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for biopsy
Prescribe oral fluconazole 50 mg daily for 7 days and review in 2 weeks
Apply topical aciclovir and review in 1 week
Reassure and advise smoking cessation; review in 3 months
Prescribe topical nystatin suspension and review in 2 weeks
correctAnswerText: Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for biopsy
explanation: The image shows oral leukoplakia: a white patch on the lateral border of the tongue that cannot be wiped off. This is a potentially malignant disorder with a 1–5% risk of transformation to oral squamous cell carcinoma. Risk factors here: age >50, heavy smoking, heavy alcohol use — all present. Option A is CORRECT. NG12 mandates an urgent 2-week-wait (2WW) referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for any unexplained white or red patch in the mouth lasting >3 weeks, particularly in patients with tobacco use, alcohol excess, or age >45. Biopsy is required to exclude dysplasia or carcinoma-in-situ. Option B (Fluconazole) is INCORRECT — oral candidiasis presents as white pseudomembranous plaques that wipe off, leaving a raw erythematous base. This lesion does NOT wipe off — the critical distinguishing feature. Option C (Topical aciclovir) is INCORRECT — leukoplakia is not viral in aetiology. Option D (Reassure + smoking cessation) is INCORRECT. Smoking cessation is essential long-term but does not substitute for urgent biopsy when a red-flag lesion is present. A 3-month delay is dangerous. Option E (Nystatin) is for oral candidiasis — incorrect diagnosis. Clinical pearl: Do NOT be reassured by the absence of pain — early oral cancer and leukoplakia with dysplasia are typically painless. The triad to memorise: non-wipeable white/red patch + >3 weeks + tobacco/alcohol = 2WW referral.

label: copd-emphysema.jpg
stem: Which of the following radiological findings is most consistent with the diagnosis of COPD/emphysema on this chest X-ray?
correctAnswerText: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened hemidiaphragms
explanation: This CXR demonstrates the hallmark features of COPD/emphysema: hyperinflated lung fields with increased lucency (air trapping), flattened hemidiaphragms (seen below the anterior end of the 6th rib), and a barrel-shaped chest. The increased AP diameter reflects chronic air trapping. Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy would suggest sarcoidosis or lymphoma. A unilateral pleural effusion is not a feature of COPD. Bilateral basal interstitial shadowing is more consistent with pulmonary fibrosis. Cardiomegaly with upper lobe diversion suggests left heart failure.

label: oral-candidiasis-thrush.jpg
stem: Examination reveals white curd-like plaques on the tongue and buccal mucosa that can be scraped off, leaving a red bleeding base. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
correctAnswerText: Fluconazole 50 mg orally once daily for 7–14 days
explanation: This image shows oral candidiasis (thrush): white curd-like plaques that scrape off to reveal a bleeding erythematous base, distinguishing thrush from leukoplakia. Option C (Fluconazole 50 mg once daily for 7–14 days) is CORRECT — /BNF first-line systemic treatment for oropharyngeal candidiasis in immunocompromised patients. Option A (Nystatin) is first-line only in mild disease in immunocompetent patients; less effective in significant immunosuppression. Option B (Fluconazole 150 mg single dose) is used for vaginal candidiasis, not oropharyngeal. Option D (Clotrimazole gel) is insufficient in immunosuppressed patients, especially with dysphagia suggesting oesophageal involvement. Option E (IV amphotericin B) is reserved for systemic/invasive or fluconazole-resistant candidiasis.

label: rosacea.jpg
stem: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his persistent facial erythema?
correctAnswerText: Topical brimonidine 0.33% gel once daily
explanation: This image shows erythematotelangiectatic rosacea (subtype I): persistent central facial erythema with telangiectasia over the nose and cheeks, without papules or pustules. Option B (Topical brimonidine 0.33% gel) is CORRECT — brimonidine is an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist causing vasoconstriction, and is /BAD first-line specifically for persistent facial erythema. It acts within 30 minutes and lasts up to 12 hours. Option A (Metronidazole) targets inflammatory rosacea with papules/pustules, not erythema alone. Option C (Doxycycline 40 mg MR) is for moderate-to-severe papulopustular rosacea. Option D (Hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED — steroids cause rebound worsening and steroid rosacea. Option E (Isotretinoin) is reserved for severe refractory rosacea. Clinical pearl: Triggers include alcohol, hot drinks, spicy food, sunlight, exercise — lifestyle modification is essential alongside treatment.

label: onychomycosis-fungal-nail-infection.jpg
stem: What is the most appropriate treatment?
correctAnswerText: Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 12–16 weeks
explanation: This image shows onychomycosis (tinea unguium): subungual hyperkeratosis with yellow-white discolouration and partial onycholysis, confirmed by Trichophyton rubrum mycology. Option C (Terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12–16 weeks) is CORRECT — /BNF first-line for dermatophyte toenail onychomycosis. Key PLAB distinction: fingernails = 6 weeks, toenails = 12–16 weeks. Check LFTs before starting. Option A (Topical amorolfine alone) is insufficient for established toenail infection with matrix involvement — topicals cannot penetrate the nail plate adequately. Option B (6 weeks) is the correct duration for fingernails only. Option D (Itraconazole pulse) is an alternative for terbinafine-intolerant patients or non-dermatophyte/Candida infections. Option E (No treatment) would be appropriate in asymptomatic elderly patients without comorbidities, but not here — diabetes increases risk of secondary bacterial infection and cellulitis. Clinical pearl: Always confirm with mycology before prescribing systemic antifungals. Psoriatic nail disease closely mimics onychomycosis.

label: atrial-flutter.jpg
stem: The ECG above is shown. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate initial management?
correctAnswerText: Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — bisoprolol 5 mg orally for rate control
explanation: This ECG shows classic atrial flutter: regular sawtooth F waves at ~300 bpm best seen in II, III, aVF and V1, with a ventricular rate of ~150 bpm due to 2:1 AV conduction. Option B (Atrial flutter 2:1 — bisoprolol orally) is CORRECT for a haemodynamically stable patient. Rate control with a beta-blocker or rate-limiting calcium channel blocker is first-line NG196. Option A (AF) is incorrect — AF is irregularly irregular with no organised atrial activity. Option C (DC cardioversion) is for haemodynamic instability or elective rhythm control, not first-line in a stable patient. Option D (Adenosine) can unmask flutter waves diagnostically but does not terminate flutter. Option E (Amiodarone for VT) is incorrect — VT is broad complex; this is narrow complex. Clinical pearl: HR of exactly 150 bpm = atrial flutter with 2:1 block until proven otherwise.

label: left-ventricular-hypertrophy-lvh.jpg
stem: The ECG above is obtained. Which of the following best describes the finding and its clinical significance?
correctAnswerText: Left ventricular hypertrophy — confirms hypertensive end-organ damage; intensify antihypertensive therapy
explanation: This ECG shows LVH: tall R waves in lateral leads (I, aVL, V4–V6), deep S waves in V1–V2, and lateral strain pattern (ST depression with T-wave inversion in V5–V6). Voltage criteria: Sokolow–Lyon: S(V1) + R(V5 or V6) ≥35 mm; Cornell: R(aVL) + S(V3) ≥28 mm (men). Option B is CORRECT — LVH is a -defined marker of hypertensive end-organ damage, mandating intensification of antihypertensives to BP <130/80 mmHg and echocardiography. Option A (RVH) shows the opposite pattern: tall R in V1, right axis deviation. Option C (LBBB) requires broad QRS >120 ms; QRS here is narrow. Option D (Anterior STEMI) — no ST elevation; the lateral ST depression is LV strain, not ischaemia. Option E (Normal) — voltage criteria clearly exceeded. Clinical pearl: LVH strain pattern mimics lateral ischaemia — distinguish by context (longstanding hypertension + voltage criteria) rather than ST changes alone.

label: geographic-tongue-benign-migratory-glossitis.jpg
stem: Examination reveals the finding shown. What is the most appropriate management?
correctAnswerText: Reassure and advise avoidance of known triggers
explanation:
This image shows geographic tongue (benign migratory glossitis): irregularly shaped erythematous patches with white serpiginous borders on the dorsal tongue, representing desquamation of filiform papillae. The map-like pattern migrates over days to weeks.

Option C is CORRECT. Geographic tongue is benign and self-limiting. Management is reassurance plus avoidance of triggers (spicy/acidic food, alcohol, tobacco).

Option A (Fluconazole) is INCORRECT — oral candidiasis presents as white pseudomembranous plaques that wipe off, leaving a raw red base; geographic tongue patches do not wipe off.

Option B (2WW referral) is INCORRECT — oral cancer red flags include indurated non-healing ulcers and persistent leukoplakia/erythroplakia >3 weeks; none present here.

Option D (Biopsy) is INCORRECT — not indicated for classic geographic tongue without suspicious features.

Option E (Topical corticosteroids) may help in severe symptomatic cases but is not first-line here.

Clinical pearl: Associated with psoriasis and atopy. Key distinction from candidiasis: candidal plaques are white and wipe off; geographic tongue has smooth red patches with white borders that cannot be removed.

label: peripheral-cyanosis.jpg
stem: What is the most appropriate immediate management?
correctAnswerText: Controlled oxygen therapy titrated to SpO₂ 88–92%, with arterial blood gas monitoring
explanation:
The image shows peripheral cyanosis: blue-purple discolouration of the nail beds and fingertips due to increased deoxygenated haemoglobin in peripheral capillaries.

Option B is CORRECT. In known COPD, controlled oxygen targeting SpO₂ 88–92% is mandated. Chronic hypercapnic COPD patients depend on hypoxic drive for ventilation — uncontrolled high-flow oxygen suppresses this drive, worsens CO₂ retention, and precipitates type 2 respiratory failure. Use a Venturi mask at 24–28% FiO₂ with ABG monitoring.

Option A (15 L/min high-flow O₂) is INCORRECT in COPD — the standard 94–98% target does NOT apply. This is potentially fatal.

Option C (Salbutamol only) is insufficient for SpO₂ 84%.

Option D (Discharge on oral prednisolone) is dangerous — this patient needs hospital admission.

Option E (CTPA) is not the immediate priority; correct hypoxaemia first.

Clinical pearl: COPD = target SpO₂ 88–92%. Everyone else = 94–98%. This is the highest-yield oxygen distinction in PLAB.

label: herpes-labialis-cold-sore.jpg
stem: The appearance is shown. What is the most appropriate management?
correctAnswerText: Topical aciclovir 5% cream applied five times daily for 5 days
explanation: The image shows herpes labialis (cold sore): painful vesicles on the vermilion border of the lip due to reactivation of latent HSV-1. Option B is CORRECT. CKS recommends topical aciclovir 5% cream five times daily for 5 days as first-line treatment in immunocompetent patients. Most effective when started at the prodromal tingling stage. Option A (Oral aciclovir) is reserved for severe/widespread HSV, immunocompromised patients, eczema herpeticum, or herpetic keratoconjunctivitis — not a straightforward cold sore. Option C (Fluconazole) is an antifungal — ineffective against HSV. Option D (Topical hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED — steroids suppress local immunity and can worsen HSV spread. Option E (Reassure only) is INCORRECT — cold sores take 7–10 days untreated; topical aciclovir shortens this. Clinical pearl: Start topical aciclovir at first tingle — before blisters appear — for maximum benefit.

label: aphthous-ulcer-mouth-ulcer.jpg
stem: The appearance is shown. What is the most appropriate initial management?
correctAnswerText: Topical chlorhexidine mouthwash and topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellet)
explanation: The image shows a minor aphthous ulcer: a well-defined round/oval shallow ulcer with a white/grey pseudomembranous base and an erythematous halo on the labial mucosa. Option A is CORRECT. CKS first-line: topical chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash (reduces secondary infection and pain) plus topical corticosteroid — hydrocortisone oromucosal 2.5 mg pellet four times daily — to shorten healing time. Option B (Oral aciclovir) is INCORRECT — aphthous ulcers are NOT caused by HSV. Herpetic ulcers are smaller, more numerous, vesicle-preceded, and occur on keratinised mucosa (hard palate, gingiva). Aphthous ulcers occur on non-keratinised mucosa (labial, buccal, floor of mouth). Option C (2WW referral) is INCORRECT — no red flags here. Urgent referral for: unexplained ulcer >3 weeks, induration, lymphadenopathy, or high-risk history (smoking, alcohol). Option D (Bloods) is appropriate for recurrent aphthous ulcers to exclude iron/B12/folate deficiency or coeliac disease, but treatment should also be started. Option E (Topical aciclovir) is INCORRECT — aciclovir is antiviral; aphthous ulcers have no viral cause. Clinical pearl: Location distinguishes aphthous from herpetic — aphthous on non-keratinised (mobile) mucosa; herpetic on keratinised (fixed) mucosa.

label: tinea-pedis-athlete-s-foot.jpg
stem: The appearance is shown. What is the first-line treatment?
correctAnswerText: Topical clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks
explanation: The image shows tinea pedis (athlete's foot): scaling, maceration, and peeling of the interdigital web spaces and plantar surface, caused by dermatophyte fungi (most commonly Trichophyton rubrum). Option A is CORRECT. Topical antifungal is first-line for tinea pedis without nail involvement. CKS recommends clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks, or terbinafine 1% cream once daily for 1 week (shorter, equally effective). Option B (Oral terbinafine) is second-line — for topical treatment failure, extensive/moccasin-type disease, or onychomycosis. Not first-line here. Option C (Topical hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED as monotherapy — steroids worsen tinea and can cause tinea incognito. Option D (Oral fluconazole) is second-line; not initial therapy for uncomplicated tinea pedis. Option E (Topical fusidic acid) is antibacterial — ineffective against dermatophytes. Clinical pearl: Consider fungal infection before prescribing topical steroids for any itchy foot rash. Hygiene advice: dry between toes, cotton socks, change footwear regularly.
